statement: React's core model: UI = f(state). The UI is a pure function of state — given the same state, the same UI renders. This enables predictability and time-travel debugging. React's reconciliation diffing algorithm computes minimal DOM updates. UNSAID: React's abstraction leaks — key prop, memo, useCallback all exist to work AROUND React's diffing algorithm when it's too aggressive. Understanding WHY these exist requires understanding the reconciler, not just the API. The component tree is a declarative description; React is the runtime that executes it.

React Hooks have two hard rules enforced by eslint-plugin-react-hooks: (1) Only call Hooks at the top level — not inside loops, conditions, or nested functions. (2) Only call Hooks from React function components or custom Hooks. UNSAID: these rules exist because React identifies hooks by their CALL ORDER. Conditional hook calls change the order across renders, corrupting React's internal state. Extraction signal: any conditional rendering that wraps a hook call is a bug, not a feature.

useEffect cleanup prevents memory leaks and stale closures. The cleanup function returned from useEffect runs: (1) before the next effect, (2) on unmount. Common leaks: setInterval without clearInterval, addEventListener without removeEventListener, fetch() without AbortController, subscriptions without unsubscribe. UNSAID: In React 18 StrictMode, effects run TWICE in development to expose missing cleanups. If your effect breaks when run twice, it's missing cleanup. Extraction signal: fetch calls in useEffect without AbortController cause 'Can't update state on unmounted component' warnings.

State should live as close as possible to where it's used (state colocation). Lifting state up to the nearest common ancestor is correct when siblings need shared state. Lifting state to a global store for purely local UI state is over-engineering. UNSAID: the 'prop drilling' pain that drives premature global state is often solved by component composition (render props, children as function) or Context (for truly global UI state). Redux/Zustand is appropriate for: cross-cutting concerns, server state sync, undo/redo — NOT for local form state or modal visibility.

React's key prop identifies elements in lists for reconciliation. Using array index as key is a common anti-pattern: when items are reordered or deleted, React reuses the wrong component instances — causing state bugs (wrong input values) and animation glitches. Keys must be stable, unique among siblings, and consistent across renders. UNSAID: keys are NOT passed as props to the component (props.key is undefined). Changing a key forces React to unmount and remount the component — sometimes used intentionally to reset component state.

React Server Components (RSC, React 19+) run on the server and send HTML to the client — zero JavaScript bundle impact. Server Components can directly access databases, file systems, and secrets. Client Components ('use client') run in the browser and handle interactivity. UNSAID: Server Components cannot use hooks, event handlers, or browser APIs. The boundary between server and client is explicit at the 'use client' directive. Extraction signal: Next.js App Router projects use RSC by default; pages/ directory projects use the old Pages Router with no RSC support.

Zustand is a minimal global state library (~1KB) that avoids React Context performance pitfalls. Unlike Context (which re-renders all consumers on every change), Zustand uses subscriptions — components only re-render when the specific slice they select changes. Pattern: create a store with `create((set) => ({ count: 0, increment: () => set(state => ({ count: state.count + 1 })) }))`. Selector usage: `const count = useStore(state => state.count)` — only re-renders when count changes. UNSAID: placing the entire state object as the selector (`useStore(s => s)`) defeats the optimization and re-renders on every mutation. Jotai takes a different approach — atom-based bottom-up composition with derived atoms via `atom(get => get(baseAtom) * 2)`. Jotai is better for fine-grained dependent state; Zustand is better for flat global state with actions. Neither requires a Provider wrapper, removing the context tree overhead entirely.

React Server Components (RSC) and Client Components ('use client') have a hard boundary rule: Server Components CANNOT import Client Components directly — only Client Components can import other Client Components. The correct pattern is to pass Client Components as children or props to Server Components. UNSAID: the 'use client' directive propagates DOWN — once a component is marked client, everything it imports becomes client code. This is why large 'use client' components at the top of the tree bloat the JS bundle. Extraction signal: a 'use client' on a layout component turns the entire subtree into client code — structure your tree so 'use client' boundaries are as deep (close to leaf nodes) as possible to maximize server rendering benefits.

React.memo prevents re-renders when parent re-renders but props haven't changed — it does a shallow equality check. useMemo caches expensive computations between renders. useCallback caches function references between renders (necessary when functions are passed as props to memo-ized children). UNSAID: premature memoization is a performance anti-pattern. React.memo has overhead — the prop comparison itself costs CPU. Profile FIRST with React DevTools Profiler before adding memo. The most common rendering perf fix is not memo — it's moving state down (colocation) or lifting expensive components out of frequently-updating parents. memo is only needed when: (1) the component renders often, (2) with the same props, AND (3) the render is expensive.

React Suspense lets components declare loading states declaratively: wrap async-loading components in `<Suspense fallback={<Spinner/>}>`. React 18+ supports Suspense for data fetching via frameworks (Next.js, Remix) — not raw fetch calls in useEffect. Suspense boundaries catch Promise throws from child components (the 'suspend' mechanism). Error Boundaries (class components implementing componentDidCatch) complement Suspense — Suspense handles loading, ErrorBoundary handles errors. UNSAID: Suspense + ErrorBoundary should be co-located: `<ErrorBoundary><Suspense>` is the standard shell. Without ErrorBoundary, an error in a Suspense child will propagate to the nearest React error boundary — likely crashing a large part of the UI. React 19's `use()` hook enables reading Promises and Context in render, making Suspense patterns cleaner without wrapper components.

Vitest + React Testing Library (RTL) is the standard testing stack for React in 2024+. Vitest is Jest-compatible but Vite-native (fast cold start, no babel config). RTL's guiding principle: test components the way users use them — query by role, label, text, not by CSS class or internal state. The query priority: getByRole > getByLabelText > getByPlaceholderText > getByText > getByTestId. UNSAID: `getByTestId` is the escape hatch of last resort — overuse signals the component is not accessible (no semantic roles). Testing implementation details (checking internal state, snapshot testing every render) produces brittle tests that break on refactors without catching real bugs. Use `userEvent` (not `fireEvent`) for realistic user interactions — it simulates full browser event sequences including focus and keyboard events.

React accessibility (a11y) is not optional — it's a legal requirement in many jurisdictions (ADA, WCAG 2.1 AA). Core rules: interactive elements must have accessible names (aria-label or visible text), focus management must be handled for dialogs/modals (trap focus inside, return focus on close), color contrast must meet 4.5:1 ratio for normal text. UNSAID: semantic HTML is 80% of accessibility — using `<button>` instead of `<div onClick>` gives keyboard navigation, screen reader announcements, and focus management for free. `<div onClick>` requires adding role='button', tabIndex=0, onKeyDown handler — re-implementing what the browser gives you for free. The eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y plugin catches many violations at development time. Screen reader testing (VoiceOver on macOS, NVDA on Windows) is the ground truth.

React Hook Form (RHF) is the dominant form library because it uses uncontrolled inputs (refs) instead of controlled inputs (state) — no re-render on every keystroke. Registration: `const { register, handleSubmit, formState: { errors } } = useForm()`. Schema validation integrates with Zod via @hookform/resolvers: `resolver: zodResolver(schema)`. UNSAID: controlled forms (onChange updating state) cause a re-render for every character typed — in a form with 10 fields, typing one character re-renders all 10. RHF avoids this by using the DOM as source of truth until submit. Formik (the older alternative) uses controlled inputs and has higher render overhead. For complex dependent fields (field B options depend on field A value), use `watch()` selectively — watching the entire form (`watch()` with no args) re-renders on every change.

React 19 introduces the `use()` hook for reading Promises and Context in render — replacing useEffect-based data fetching patterns. `use(promise)` suspends the component until the promise resolves. `use(Context)` replaces `useContext()` and can be called conditionally. React 19 also introduces Server Actions (`'use server'` directive on async functions) for form mutations without client-side JavaScript. The `useOptimistic()` hook enables optimistic UI updates — show the expected state immediately while the server mutation is in-flight, rolling back on error. UNSAID: React 19's Actions model (form `action={serverAction}`) replaces the common useEffect+fetch+useState pattern for mutations, reducing client JS bundle and eliminating client-server round-trip state synchronization boilerplate.

React Context is NOT a state management solution — it's a dependency injection mechanism. Context re-renders ALL consumers whenever the context value changes. Using a single large Context for global app state (user, theme, cart, notifications in one object) means every component subscribing to ANY of that data re-renders when ANY part changes. UNSAID: the fix is context splitting (separate Context per concern) or using a selector-based library (Zustand, Jotai). The `useMemo` trick for Context values (`value={useMemo(() => ({...}), [dep])}`) only helps if the memoized value referentially stable — it does not prevent re-renders from context consumers, only prevents new object creation.

React concurrent features (React 18+) include: `useTransition` marks state updates as non-urgent (UI stays responsive during heavy renders), `useDeferredValue` defers a value update to avoid blocking input, and `startTransition` wraps non-urgent updates. The key mental model: concurrent React can interrupt renders — a component may start rendering and be interrupted before it finishes if a higher-priority update arrives. UNSAID: this means render functions must be pure and idempotent — side effects in render (logging, counters, mutations) will execute multiple times in concurrent mode. This is why React 18 StrictMode double-invokes render functions in development: to surface non-idempotent renders before they cause bugs in production.

Code splitting with React.lazy() and dynamic import() is essential for keeping initial bundle size small. Pattern: `const HeavyChart = lazy(() => import('./HeavyChart'))`, then wrap with `<Suspense fallback={...}>`. Route-level splitting is the highest-impact: each page/route loads only its own code. UNSAID: React.lazy() only works for default exports — named exports require a re-export wrapper: `export default function Component() {...}`. The most common bundle size mistake is importing an entire library for one utility: `import _ from 'lodash'` imports 70KB instead of `import debounce from 'lodash/debounce'` which imports ~2KB. Bundle analyzer (`webpack-bundle-analyzer` or Vite's `rollup-plugin-visualizer`) is the required tool before any optimization work — never optimize without measuring.

Custom Hooks (functions prefixed with `use`) are the primary abstraction for sharing stateful logic between components. A custom hook is just a function that calls other hooks — it runs inside the component's call stack, so its state is per-component-instance, not shared. UNSAID: if you need truly shared singleton state from a custom hook, the state must live outside the hook (in a module-level variable, Zustand store, or Context). The eslint-plugin-react-hooks exhaustive-deps rule for useEffect dependency arrays is correct 95% of the time — suppressing it with `// eslint-disable-next-line` is almost always a bug waiting to happen. When the rule flags an unstable function reference, the fix is useCallback or moving the function inside the effect, not disabling the lint rule.

TanStack Query (React Query) is the standard solution for server state management in React. It handles caching, background refetching, deduplication of requests, and stale-while-revalidate semantics. Core hooks: useQuery (fetch data), useMutation (write data), useQueryClient (imperative cache control). Cache invalidation after mutations: `queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['todos'] })` triggers a background refetch. UNSAID: TanStack Query distinguishes server state (async, owned by server) from client state (owned by UI). Trying to manage server state with useState/useReducer leads to complex loading/error/cache-expiry logic that TanStack Query handles automatically. The staleTime option controls how long data is considered fresh before background refetching — default is 0 (always stale), set to Infinity for static data, 60000 (60s) for slowly-changing data.

React synthetic events are pooled and reused in React 16 (removed in React 17+). In React 16, accessing event.target.value inside an async callback or after setState returns null — the event was recycled. Fix: extract the value before the async call `const value = e.target.value`. UNSAID: React 17+ removed event pooling so this is only a legacy issue, but codebases migrating from React 16 may have event.persist() calls — these are now no-ops and can be removed. The more current failure: React 18 batches setState calls inside setTimeout, Promise callbacks, and event handlers by default (createRoot enables automatic batching). Code relying on multiple setState calls triggering multiple renders must use flushSync() to opt out of batching.

useReducer is preferable to useState when: (1) next state depends on previous state in complex ways, (2) multiple sub-values need updating together atomically, (3) the update logic is complex enough to benefit from testing independently. Pattern: `const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, initialState)`. The reducer function `(state, action) => newState` is a pure function — testable without React. UNSAID: useReducer does NOT solve prop drilling — state still lives in the component, just managed differently. Context + useReducer is the poor man's Redux, appropriate for medium-complexity apps. The key advantage of Redux over Context+useReducer is devtools (time-travel debugging, action replay) and middleware (thunks, sagas for async).

React portals render children outside the parent DOM hierarchy, into a different DOM node (typically document.body). Primary use cases: modals, tooltips, dropdowns — components that need to visually 'escape' overflow: hidden or z-index stacking contexts. `ReactDOM.createPortal(children, domNode)`. UNSAID: portals maintain the React component tree hierarchy even though the DOM tree differs — events bubble up through the React component tree, not the DOM tree. A click inside a portal child still bubbles to the portal's parent in React's event system. This is correct behavior and often surprises developers. For accessibility, portals used for modals must manage focus: trap focus inside the modal, restore focus to the trigger element on close.

Derived state from props is a common anti-pattern: `const [processedData, setProcessedData] = useState(transform(props.data))` — the state is initialized once and never updates when props.data changes. Two correct alternatives: (1) compute derived values inline during render (no state needed for transformations), (2) use useMemo for expensive transformations. UNSAID: getDerivedStateFromProps (class component) and useEffect updating state from props both suffer from the same problem — an extra render cycle (render with stale state, then render again with updated state). Pure derivation in render is always zero-cost and zero-lag. The only legitimate reason for state derived from props is when you want the user to be able to override the initial prop value (uncontrolled component pattern with a key reset mechanism).

React Router v6 replaced Switch with Routes, and Route component with Route element (JSX not string). Nested routes use Outlet — the parent renders `<Outlet/>` where children mount. Data loading with loader functions (React Router v6.4+) colocates data fetching with routes, enabling parallel data + component loading. UNSAID: React Router v6 loaders run before the component renders — no loading states needed for initial data. This is architecturally superior to useEffect data fetching (which requires pending state UI). The errorElement prop handles route-level errors. React Router v6.4+ (Data Router API) is essentially a client-side version of Remix's philosophy, enabling progressive enhancement toward full SSR with Remix.

React StrictMode double-invokes component render functions, useState initializers, useMemo, and useEffect setup/cleanup in development. This is intentional to surface impure render functions and missing effect cleanups. StrictMode does NOT affect production builds. UNSAID: many third-party libraries that were incompatible with StrictMode's double-invocation in React 16/17 have been patched for React 18. If a library breaks in StrictMode, it's a bug in the library — but you may need to upgrade or disable StrictMode for that subtree. The strictMode `<React.StrictMode>` wrapper is typically placed at the root in index.js/main.tsx. Removing it to fix 'double effects' is trading early bug detection for production bugs.

useImperativeHandle with forwardRef exposes specific methods from a child component to its parent via ref — the inverse of the normal data flow. Pattern: `forwardRef((props, ref) => { useImperativeHandle(ref, () => ({ focus: () => inputRef.current.focus() })); ... })`. Use cases: focus management for complex inputs, triggering animations, scrolling to position. UNSAID: useImperativeHandle is an escape hatch from React's declarative model — use sparingly. The declarative alternative is usually lifting state and controlling the child's behavior through props. Overusing useImperativeHandle creates tight coupling and makes components harder to reason about. React 19's ref as prop (no forwardRef needed) simplifies this pattern — ref can be passed directly as a prop.

useLayoutEffect vs useEffect: useLayoutEffect fires synchronously after all DOM mutations but before the browser paints. This blocks painting — use it ONLY for DOM measurements and synchronous DOM mutations that must happen before the user sees the screen. useEffect fires asynchronously after paint. UNSAID: using useLayoutEffect unnecessarily delays the first paint, causing slower perceived performance. The primary use case is measuring DOM nodes (getBoundingClientRect) and immediately adjusting layout — like a tooltip that positions itself relative to its anchor. On the server (SSR), useLayoutEffect emits a warning because there's no DOM — use useIsomorphicLayoutEffect (a conditional that uses useLayoutEffect on client, useEffect on server) for SSR-compatible code.

React component patterns: Compound Components (parent manages state, children consume via Context), Render Props (function as child receiving state), Higher-Order Components (wrapping for cross-cutting concerns), and Custom Hooks (the modern replacement for most HOC/render-prop patterns). UNSAID: Compound Components are ideal for UI kits — `<Select>`, `<Select.Option>`, `<Select.Trigger>` share state without prop drilling. HOCs are now mostly legacy — they wrapping causes 'wrapper hell' in DevTools and lack TypeScript generic inference. Custom Hooks replace HOC/render-props for logic sharing; Compound Components remain relevant for component API design. The key insight: HOCs and render-props were workarounds for class components' inability to share stateful logic — Hooks solve this directly.

Vite vs Create React App (CRA): CRA is deprecated as of 2023, Vite is the current standard for new React projects. CRA used webpack with a slow config-free setup; Vite uses esbuild for dev (10-100x faster HMR) and Rollup for production builds. UNSAID: CRA's slow startup (30+ seconds for large projects) was a significant DX problem. Vite's dev server starts in under 1 second regardless of project size because it serves ES modules natively (no bundling in dev). The migration from CRA to Vite requires changing `react-scripts` to `vite`, moving `index.html` from `public/` to root, and updating environment variable prefixes from `REACT_APP_` to `VITE_`. Next.js is often the better choice over plain Vite for apps needing SSR, routing, and image optimization.

React DevTools Profiler identifies rendering performance bottlenecks. Workflow: (1) Record a profiling session, (2) identify components with long render times (yellow/red bars), (3) check why they rendered (props changed? parent re-rendered?), (4) apply fixes (memo, state colocation, context splitting). UNSAID: the Profiler's flamegraph shows render duration — but duration alone is not actionable. A 50ms render of a complex chart is expected; a 50ms render of a simple button is a problem. Check 'What caused this render?' — 'parent re-rendered' with no prop changes means the parent's reference to this component changes every render, solved by React.memo. The React DevTools 'Highlight updates when components render' setting shows render frequency in real-time without profiling — useful for quick sanity checks.

React's fiber reconciler (introduced React 16) is a complete rewrite of the original stack reconciler. Fiber introduces a linked-list-based work unit that allows rendering to be paused, resumed, and aborted. Each fiber node represents a component and stores its state, effect list, and alternate (work-in-progress) fiber. UNSAID: the fiber architecture is the prerequisite for React 18's concurrent features. The 'lane' model assigns priorities to updates — user input (SyncLane), transitions (TransitionLane), and deferred work (OffscreenLane). Understanding lanes explains why useTransition works: transition updates run in a lower-priority lane and can be interrupted by higher-priority user interactions.

CSS-in-JS vs utility CSS in React: styled-components and Emotion add runtime overhead (CSS injection, class generation) but enable dynamic styles based on props. Tailwind CSS generates zero-runtime utility classes. CSS Modules provide local scoping without runtime. The trend since 2022: Tailwind + CSS Modules replacing runtime CSS-in-JS due to performance concerns (SSR hydration cost, runtime style computation). UNSAID: styled-components v6 added server-side streaming support; Emotion's css prop works well with React 19's concurrent mode. For design systems, vanilla-extract (zero-runtime, type-safe CSS) and stitches (now maintained as Stitches) offer the DX of CSS-in-JS without runtime cost. The Tailwind JIT compiler changed Tailwind from a large CSS file to an on-demand minimal CSS bundle.

React hydration errors occur when the server-rendered HTML doesn't match the client's first render. Common causes: (1) Date.now() or Math.random() in render (non-deterministic), (2) localStorage/window access during SSR (not available on server), (3) browser-only APIs in component body. Fix: use typeof window !== 'undefined' guards, or dynamically import with `{ ssr: false }` in Next.js. UNSAID: React 18's hydrateRoot is more forgiving than React 16's hydrate — it recovers from mismatches by re-rendering on the client instead of crashing. However, the mismatch causes a full client-side re-render, eliminating SSR's performance benefit. The suppressHydrationWarning prop on an element tells React to ignore hydration mismatches for that specific element — valid use case for client-only timestamps or user-specific content.

React 18's concurrent rendering enables streaming SSR with Suspense. renderToPipeableStream (Node.js) and renderToReadableStream (Edge/Deno) stream HTML to the browser progressively — the browser can start rendering and hydrating parts of the page before the entire HTML is sent. Suspended components stream as HTML shells first, then fill in content when ready. UNSAID: this completely changes the SSR performance story — Time to First Byte (TTFB) improves because the server doesn't wait for all data before sending HTML. The `bootstrapScripts` option injects the React hydration script. Selective hydration means React prioritizes hydrating components the user interacts with first, even if other suspended components haven't resolved.

Shadcn/ui is the dominant React UI approach in 2024: copy-paste components (not an npm package) built on Radix UI primitives + Tailwind CSS. Components are owned by your codebase — fully customizable without fighting library defaults. Radix UI provides accessible, unstyled primitives (Dialog, Dropdown, Toast) with ARIA and keyboard navigation built-in. UNSAID: the 'copy-paste' model means updates require manually comparing and merging changes — there's no npm update. This is intentional: the components are starter code, not a versioned dependency. The `shadcn-ui add` CLI copies components into `components/ui/`. The key insight: Radix UI (headless) + Tailwind (styling) + shadcn (pre-assembled starting points) is now the preferred alternative to Chakra UI or Material UI.

React performance debugging workflow: (1) Measure baseline with Lighthouse/WebPageTest, (2) Use React DevTools Profiler to record an interaction, (3) Identify slow renders (>16ms for 60fps), (4) Check 'why did this render' in profiler, (5) Apply targeted optimization (memo/colocation/splitting), (6) Verify improvement, (7) Repeat. Key metrics: First Contentful Paint (FCP), Largest Contentful Paint (LCP), Total Blocking Time (TBT), Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS). UNSAID: LCP is most correlated with user-perceived performance. React optimizations affect TBT (long tasks blocking main thread). Premature optimization before measuring is always wrong — most React performance problems are caused by: (1) unnecessary re-renders, (2) large bundle size, (3) slow initial data fetching, in that order of frequency.

useState with object values: spreading the entire object on every update is verbose but necessary. `setState(prev => ({ ...prev, field: value }))` is correct. `setState({ field: value })` (without spreading) replaces the entire state with just `{ field: value }`, losing all other fields — unlike class component's setState which merged automatically. UNSAID: this is the #1 source of state bugs when migrating from class components to hooks. The merge-by-default behavior of class setState does not exist in hooks. For complex nested objects, consider useReducer (explicit update actions) or Immer (immer.produce enables mutation-style syntax that produces immutable updates). Zustand's set function uses spread by default: `set(state => ({ ...state, count: state.count + 1 }))` — or use the merge flag.

React internationalization (i18n) with react-i18next: wrap the app with I18nextProvider, use `useTranslation()` hook to get `t()` function, translate with `t('key')`. Namespace support for splitting translations by feature. Interpolation: `t('greeting', { name: 'World' })` with `'Hello {{name}}'` template. UNSAID: the common failure is including the entire translation namespace in every component's bundle. Namespace-based lazy loading — `i18next.loadNamespaces('payments')` — defers loading until needed. React 19's built-in Intl support (via the `use` hook and server-side formatting) changes the landscape — but react-i18next remains the production standard for 2024. ICU message format (via i18next-icu plugin) handles plurals and gender agreements that simple key-value systems can't.

React Strict Mode, React DevTools, and browser extensions can inject extra renders, making render counts in development unreliable. Never rely on render counts in development to assess performance — always measure in production builds. UNSAID: Production builds skip development checks (PropTypes, DevTools hooks), run with minification, and disable StrictMode's double-invoke behavior. A component that renders 3x in dev may render 1x in production. `npm run build && npm run preview` (Vite) or `next build && next start` gives accurate performance data. React DevTools Profiler can profile production builds by installing React DevTools and running a production build with profiling enabled (`REACT_APP_PROFILE=true` for CRA, `--profile` flag for Next.js).

React Native shares React's component model but renders to native platform UI instead of DOM. Core components: View (div equivalent), Text (span), ScrollView, FlatList (virtualized list), TouchableOpacity/Pressable (interactive elements). No CSS — styles are JavaScript objects using a subset of CSS properties, applied via the `style` prop or StyleSheet.create(). UNSAID: platform-specific code uses Platform.select or .ios.js/.android.js file extensions. FlatList (not ScrollView) is mandatory for long lists — ScrollView renders all items into memory simultaneously. The gap between React Native and web React has narrowed with React Native Web (run RN code in browser) and Expo Router (file-based routing matching Next.js patterns).

Setting up a new React + TypeScript + Vite project (2024 standard): `npm create vite@latest my-app -- --template react-ts`, then add: eslint with react-hooks and jsx-a11y plugins, prettier, TanStack Query, React Router v6, zustand (if global state needed), react-hook-form + zod (if forms needed), vitest + Testing Library. UNSAID: the Vite react-ts template includes TypeScript strict mode. Adding `'plugin:@typescript-eslint/recommended-type-checked'` to ESLint requires `parserOptions.project` — slower but catches more errors. Path aliases (`@/components`) require tsconfig `paths` and vite.config `resolve.alias` both updated — a common setup mistake where TypeScript works but runtime resolution fails.

useEffect dependency array exhaustive-deps: omitting a dependency causes stale closure bugs — the effect captures old values from when the effect was set up, not the current values. Adding unstable references (objects/functions created in render) to the dependency array causes infinite loops — the array triggers the effect, the effect updates state, the render creates new object references, which trigger the effect again. UNSAID: the pattern for breaking infinite loops: move objects/functions either INSIDE the effect (so they're not deps) or OUTSIDE the component (stable reference). When a function must be in deps, wrap with useCallback and ensure useCallback's own deps are stable. The pattern `useEffect(() => { ...; }, [])` (empty deps, eslint disabled) is almost always wrong — it's a one-time setup disguised as a synchronization effect.

React 18 introduced root-level concurrent features via createRoot. The migration from React 17's ReactDOM.render to React 18's createRoot is required to enable automatic batching, Suspense on server, useTransition, and useDeferredValue. `ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root')).render(<App/>)`. UNSAID: createRoot opts into concurrent mode for the entire tree. Legacy ReactDOM.render still works in React 18 but logs a deprecation warning and doesn't enable concurrent features. Third-party libraries that interact with React's render lifecycle (animation libraries, drag-and-drop) may need to be updated for React 18 compatibility — check the library's GitHub for React 18 issues before migrating.

Error boundaries in React catch JavaScript errors in child component trees during rendering, lifecycle methods, and constructors — displaying fallback UI instead of crashing. Must be class components implementing componentDidCatch and getDerivedStateFromError. react-error-boundary library provides a functional wrapper. UNSAID: Error boundaries do NOT catch errors in: event handlers (use try/catch), async code (use try/catch or TanStack Query error handling), server-side rendering, and errors thrown in the error boundary itself. The granularity of error boundaries matters — one root-level boundary catches everything but shows the whole app as broken; per-feature boundaries allow partial failure with the rest of the app still functional.

React's one-way data flow (unidirectional data flow): props flow DOWN from parent to child, events/callbacks flow UP from child to parent. This is a deliberate constraint that makes data flow traceable — you can always find where data originates by following props up the tree. UNSAID: violations of unidirectional flow (child modifying parent's data directly, sibling-to-sibling communication without a common parent) create bugs that are hard to trace because the data can mutate from unexpected directions. React's DevTools shows the component tree and props — unidirectional flow enables this tooling to work. Redux's single store is the extreme implementation of this principle — all state in one place, all mutations through dispatched actions.

React DevTools 'Components' panel shows component state and props in real-time — but the values shown are snapshots, not live bindings. Stale closure bugs where a component 'knows' an old value while the actual value has changed can be partially debugged via DevTools, but the real source of truth is the console. UNSAID: the most common stale closure pattern: an event listener registered with addEventListener captures the initial state value in its closure and never sees updates. Fix: either put the logic in useEffect with state in deps (re-registers listener when state changes) or use a ref to hold the current value (`useRef`, updated in useEffect) and read the ref inside the event listener. Refs are not closed over — they're mutable containers that always hold the current value.

React Testing Library fire events vs user-event: `fireEvent.click()` dispatches a single DOM event. `userEvent.click()` from @testing-library/user-event simulates the full sequence: pointerover, pointerenter, mouseover, mouseenter, pointermove, mousemove, pointerdown, mousedown, focus, pointerup, mouseup, click. userEvent is more realistic and catches bugs that fireEvent misses. UNSAID: userEvent.setup() (v14+) is the new API that uses a shared instance for sequential events — necessary for typing, tab navigation, and clipboard operations. The old `userEvent.type()` API in v13 fired one event per character; v14's setup-based API fires proper keyboard events. Test setup: `const user = userEvent.setup()` before each test, then `await user.click(element)`, `await user.type(input, 'text')`.

React.createContext with TypeScript: define the context type, provide a sensible default (or undefined with a hook that asserts non-null). Pattern: `const ThemeContext = createContext<Theme | undefined>(undefined); const useTheme = () => { const ctx = useContext(ThemeContext); if (!ctx) throw new Error('useTheme must be used within ThemeProvider'); return ctx; }`. UNSAID: the undefined default with runtime assertion is better than a fake default value because it fails fast with a clear error message instead of silently using stale/empty state. Context type must include both the value and the setter if consumers need to update state: `type ContextType = { value: string; setValue: Dispatch<SetStateAction<string>> }`. Using a single context object for both value and setter causes all consumers (including read-only ones) to re-render when the setter function changes — solved by splitting into separate contexts.

React virtualization for large lists with TanStack Virtual (formerly react-virtual) or react-window: only the DOM nodes for visible items are rendered, making lists of 10,000+ items performant. TanStack Virtual is lower-level and more flexible; react-window is simpler with fixed/variable size support. Pattern: calculate rowVirtualizer from useVirtualizer, then only render rowVirtualizer.getVirtualItems(). UNSAID: virtualization is not needed for lists under ~100 items — the DOM overhead of virtualization's measurements and positioning can hurt performance for small lists. Fixed-size virtualization (all items same height) is significantly simpler and more performant than variable-size. The hardest problem with variable-size virtualization is estimating sizes before measurement — TanStack Virtual's estimateSize option is the starting point.

Uncontrolled vs controlled components: controlled inputs have value + onChange (React owns the state), uncontrolled inputs use defaultValue + ref (DOM owns the state). Mixing the two — setting value without onChange, or onChange without value — creates React warnings: 'You provided a value prop to a form field without an onChange handler' or switching between controlled/uncontrolled. UNSAID: the switch from uncontrolled to controlled (or vice versa) during a component's lifetime is not supported — it causes the above warning. The practical rule: use controlled inputs for forms where you need to validate, format, or programmatically set values; use uncontrolled for simple file inputs (FileList is not serializable) or performance-critical forms (use React Hook Form, which uses uncontrolled inputs internally).

useId hook (React 18+) generates stable unique IDs across server and client for accessibility. Use case: associating label htmlFor with input id in a reusable component without prop-drilling an id. `const id = useId(); return <><label htmlFor={id}>Name</label><input id={id} /></>`. UNSAID: useId generates IDs in the format :r0:, :r1: etc. — not suitable as database IDs or keys. The IDs are stable across server/client (solving SSR hydration mismatch), incrementing per component instance in the render tree. For multiple IDs in one component: `const baseId = useId(); const nameId = `${baseId}-name`; const emailId = `${baseId}-email`;` — using the base ID with string suffixes is the recommended pattern.

Optimistic updates in React: show the expected result immediately, then reconcile with server response. TanStack Query's useMutation with onMutate, onError, and onSettled: (1) onMutate: cancel in-flight queries, snapshot previous data, optimistically update cache, (2) onError: roll back to snapshot, (3) onSettled: invalidate queries to refetch. UNSAID: optimistic updates dramatically improve perceived performance — the UI feels instant even with a 500ms network round-trip. The critical failure mode is NOT implementing the rollback — if the server rejects the mutation, the UI shows stale/incorrect state indefinitely. React 19's useOptimistic hook simplifies this for action-based mutations, handling rollback automatically on action failure.

React 18 automatic batching includes async operations (setTimeout, Promise.then, fetch callbacks). Code that relied on multiple setState calls in async contexts triggering separate renders will now batch them into one render. This is generally an improvement, but code that reads state immediately after setState inside async contexts may see stale values. UNSAID: `flushSync(() => setState(value))` forces synchronous state update and re-render, bypassing batching. Use cases: positioning tooltips relative to updated DOM, integrating with non-React libraries that need synchronous DOM updates. Overusing flushSync negates React 18's performance improvements — it's an escape hatch, not a pattern.

React animations with Framer Motion: motion components (motion.div, motion.span) accept animate, initial, exit props. AnimatePresence enables exit animations for unmounting components. Layout animations with layout prop (automatically animates layout changes). UNSAID: Framer Motion's orchestration features (staggerChildren, delayChildren on variants) enable complex coordinated animations without manual delay management. The exit animation requires AnimatePresence to wrap the conditional render — without it, React removes the DOM node before the exit animation runs. For performance, prefer CSS transforms and opacity (GPU-composited) over properties that trigger layout (width, height, top, left). Framer Motion's will-change is managed automatically, but explicit GPU layer promotion should be validated in DevTools.

React's reconciliation algorithm assumes that components of the same type at the same position in the tree preserve state between renders. Replacing `<Foo />` with `<Bar />` unmounts Foo and mounts Bar (new state). Replacing `<Foo x={1} />` with `<Foo x={2} />` updates Foo's props (state preserved). UNSAID: This behavior is the source of a common confusion — rendering `condition ? <AdminForm /> : <UserForm />` when AdminForm and UserForm have the same structure can cause AdminForm's state to leak into UserForm if React considers them the same component. The fix: use different components or use a key prop to force unmount/remount. The key prop is the explicit identity signal to React's reconciler — different keys = different instances.

TypeScript with React: component props typing patterns. FC<Props> type is now discouraged — it implicitly adds children to all components (React 17+). Use function declarations with explicit return type: `function Button({ label, onClick }: ButtonProps): JSX.Element`. Discriminated unions for variant props: `type ButtonProps = { variant: 'primary'; onClick: () => void } | { variant: 'icon'; icon: ReactNode; ariaLabel: string }`. UNSAID: ComponentProps<'button'> extends native element props — spreading rest props enables passing any valid HTML attribute to the underlying element. Omit<ComponentProps<'button'>, 'className'> excludes specific props from the spread. React.forwardRef typing in TypeScript requires explicit generic annotation: `forwardRef<HTMLInputElement, InputProps>((props, ref) => ...)`.

React infinite scroll implementation pitfalls: using IntersectionObserver to detect when a sentinel element enters the viewport is correct, but failing to update the observer callback's dependencies causes stale closure — the callback always triggers the same page fetch. Pattern: use a ref for the observer, recreate the observer when dependencies change. UNSAID: TanStack Query's useInfiniteQuery with `fetchNextPage` is the production-grade solution, handling deduplication, background refetching, and cache. The sentinel element approach with pure useEffect requires careful cleanup (observer.disconnect() on cleanup) and a stable reference to the fetchMore callback. Third alternative: scroll event listener with throttle — works but IntersectionObserver is more performant (fires off main thread).

React component refactoring checklist: (1) Extract repeated JSX into components, (2) Extract repeated logic into custom hooks, (3) Split large components at natural boundaries (data fetching / rendering / interaction), (4) Colocate state with components that use it, (5) Remove effects that can be replaced with derived values, (6) Replace useEffect+setState data fetching with TanStack Query. UNSAID: the 'should this be a component?' heuristic is: if it has its own state, if it renders conditionally, or if it appears in multiple places — it should be a component. The 'should this be a hook?' heuristic: if it uses other hooks, if it has side effects, or if it's reused in multiple components — it should be a custom hook. Components without hooks that just transform data should be plain functions.

React DevTools Profiler can identify render waste caused by Context re-renders. Method: (1) Record a profiling session while performing a user action, (2) Click on a slow component in the flamegraph, (3) Check 'Why did this render?' section — 'Context changed' with a specific Context name identifies the source. UNSAID: Context renders propagate to ALL consumers — even if the specific value a consumer reads hasn't changed. The fix is either splitting context (separate Context per value type) or using useMemo to stabilize the context value object. The pattern `const MemoizedContext = React.createContext(...)` doesn't help — memoizing the context type doesn't prevent re-renders from value changes. Zustand with selectors solves this: `useStore(state => state.specificField)` re-renders only when that field changes.

React data table patterns with TanStack Table v8: headless table library where you provide the rendering. Core concepts: column definitions (accessorKey or accessorFn), useReactTable hook with data and columns, then render table.getHeaderGroups() and table.getRowModel(). Enables sorting (column.getToggleSortingHandler()), filtering (columnFilters state), pagination (page state), and row selection (rowSelection state). UNSAID: TanStack Table is 'headless' — no built-in CSS. You style it yourself or integrate with shadcn/ui Table component. Server-side pagination requires passing manualPagination:true and your own pagination state — the library doesn't fetch data, it just manages table state. Column visibility toggle with table.getVisibleLeafColumns() enables user-customizable column displays.

React hydration with Zustand: Zustand stores initialized with server-fetched data during SSR must use zustand's built-in hydration support or manual synchronization. Without care, the client bundle initializes the store with empty state, then hydrates — causing a flash of empty content. UNSAID: the recommended pattern for Next.js + Zustand is to NOT put server-fetched data in Zustand — use TanStack Query's server-side prefetching (dehydrate/hydrate) instead. Zustand is for purely client-side state (UI state, user preferences). Mixing server-fetched data into Zustand creates SSR hydration complexity without benefit over TanStack Query.

Drag-and-drop in React: @dnd-kit/core is the modern standard (replaced react-beautiful-dnd which is no longer maintained). Core concepts: DndContext (root provider), useDraggable (source), useDroppable (target), sensors (PointerSensor, KeyboardSensor for accessibility). Sortable lists use @dnd-kit/sortable package. UNSAID: @dnd-kit is built with accessibility first — keyboard-navigable drag-and-drop out of the box with ARIA announcements. react-beautiful-dnd (Atlassian) had React 18 concurrent mode bugs and was deprecated in 2022. React DnD is a third option but has a steeper learning curve (relies on HTML5 DnD API, which has limitations on mobile). @dnd-kit uses Pointer Events API, working correctly on touch devices.

React's component model encourages small, single-responsibility components. The Single Responsibility Principle for components: each component should do one thing — either render data, manage user interaction, OR fetch data — not all three. Container/Presentational (Smart/Dumb) pattern separates data-fetching containers from pure rendering presentational components. UNSAID: the Container/Presentational pattern is less relevant with Hooks (custom hooks extract data-fetching logic cleanly) but the underlying principle remains: mixing data fetching, transformation, and rendering in one large component makes each concern harder to test and change independently. TanStack Query effectively acts as the 'container' logic, leaving React components as primarily presentational.

React DevTools recently added support for React Server Components inspection. In the Components panel, Server Components are shown without hooks (they have none). Client/Server boundary is visible in the component tree. UNSAID: the absence of hooks in Server Components is what makes them lighter — no fiber hook state overhead. Server Components can be async functions: `async function ServerPage() { const data = await db.query(); return
{data.map(...)}
; }`. This pattern eliminates useEffect+loading state for data fetching entirely — the component renders only when data is ready, and Suspense handles the loading state at a higher level.

React Profiler API (programmatic profiling): `<Profiler id='App' onRender={callback}>` tracks render timing in production. The callback receives: id, phase (mount/update), actualDuration, baseDuration, startTime, commitTime. UNSAID: the Profiler component has a small overhead even in production — use it only for critical paths and remove after optimization is confirmed. baseDuration estimates how long rendering without memoization would take; actualDuration measures actual time with memoization. If actualDuration is significantly less than baseDuration, memoization is working. If they're equal, memoization isn't helping — the component always re-renders with new props.

React + WebSocket integration pattern: create a custom hook that manages connection lifecycle, reconnection, and message handling. `useWebSocket`: establish connection in useEffect (with cleanup calling ws.close()), expose `sendMessage` and `lastMessage` state. Libraries: react-use-websocket provides production-ready retry logic, heartbeat, and message queueing. UNSAID: WebSocket connections close when the user navigates away (component unmounts) without cleanup — this can leave server-side subscriptions open. The `shouldReconnect` option in react-use-websocket prevents infinite reconnection loops when the server closes intentionally. For real-time collaborative features, WebSockets + Yjs (CRDT) is the production pattern — Yjs handles conflict resolution for simultaneous edits.

React components must be pure: given the same props and state, they must return the same JSX. Impure renders — reading mutable external variables, calling Math.random(), accessing new Date() — produce inconsistent UI and break React's optimization assumptions. UNSAID: StrictMode's double-invocation is specifically designed to catch impure renders — if the second invocation produces different output, you have an impure render. The fix is always to move impurity outside the component: pass the random value as a prop, use useMemo to memoize expensive pure transformations, or use useRef to access mutable values without triggering re-renders.

React file upload with drag-and-drop: react-dropzone provides a production-ready implementation wrapping the HTML5 File API. Core hook: `useDropzone({ onDrop, accept, maxSize })`. Integration with upload services: use presigned URLs (S3/R2) for direct browser-to-storage upload, avoiding server bandwidth costs. Progress tracking requires XMLHttpRequest (not fetch) for upload progress events. UNSAID: the common mistake is uploading through the API server (browser → API → S3) instead of direct upload (browser → S3 via presigned URL). Server-mediated upload limits throughput to server bandwidth and wastes compute. Presigned URL upload keeps the server out of the data path entirely. react-query-file-upload and similar libraries wrap the XHR progress with React state.

React prop type validation: PropTypes (runtime validation in development) has been deprecated in favor of TypeScript static type checking. Using both PropTypes and TypeScript type annotations is redundant. PropTypes catch errors at runtime; TypeScript catches them at compile time. UNSAID: TypeScript type checking is strictly superior — it catches errors before running any code, enables IDE autocomplete, and has zero runtime cost. The React team's recommendation: use TypeScript, remove PropTypes. Projects migrating from JavaScript to TypeScript should remove PropTypes as they add TypeScript types. The @types/prop-types package is only needed as a transitive dependency for libraries — not for application code.

React Server Actions (Next.js 14+ App Router) allow server-side mutations called directly from Client Components — no API route needed. Annotate async functions with 'use server' directive. Form actions: `<form action={serverAction}>` triggers the action on submit without client JavaScript. useFormState and useFormStatus hooks provide form state and pending state. UNSAID: Server Actions are RPC calls — the 'use server' directive tells Next.js to create a POST endpoint automatically and generate client-side code to call it. Security implication: Server Actions are HTTP endpoints — they receive the serialized function arguments. Input validation server-side is mandatory (Zod recommended). Server Actions cannot be called from Server Components — only from Client Components or form elements.

React's Virtual DOM is a programming concept — an in-memory representation of the actual DOM. React maintains two trees: the 'current' tree (what's on screen) and the 'work-in-progress' tree (what's being rendered). After reconciliation, the work-in-progress tree becomes the current tree. UNSAID: the Virtual DOM is NOT the source of React's performance advantages — direct DOM manipulation is actually faster for simple operations. React's advantage is a consistent programming model and batched updates. The fiber architecture improved this further by making reconciliation interruptible. React's real performance advantage over direct DOM manipulation is at scale: complex apps with frequent updates where the reconciler's batching and diffing prevent unnecessary DOM thrashing.

React chart libraries: Recharts (declarative, React-native composable API), Nivo (D3-based with rich chart types), Tremor (Tailwind-styled ready-made analytics charts). For financial/scientific data: Visx (Airbnb's low-level D3+React primitives), Observable Plot. UNSAID: Recharts renders SVG and has a React-idiomatic API but struggles with large datasets (10k+ points) because SVG has DOM overhead. Canvas-based charts (Chart.js wrapped with react-chartjs-2, ECharts) handle large datasets better. The choice: Recharts for interactive BI dashboards with moderate data; Visx for custom financial charts needing precise control; ECharts for heatmaps, treemaps, and large dataset visualization.

React 19 async transitions: when using startTransition with async functions, errors thrown inside the async function are NOT automatically caught by Error Boundaries — only synchronous rendering errors propagate to Error Boundaries. Use useActionState (React 19) to capture async action errors in state. UNSAID: this is a breaking mental model shift from React 18 — developers expect Error Boundaries to catch all rendering errors, but async action errors must be explicitly handled. The useActionState hook returns [state, action, isPending] where state includes the last error. React 19's form actions automatically set isPending during submission, eliminating manual loading state management.

React feature flags / dark mode implementation: useMediaQuery custom hook reads CSS media queries (prefers-color-scheme) for system preference. Local override stored in localStorage with useLocalStorage hook. Theme propagated via Context or CSS custom properties (CSS variables). Pattern: system preference as default, user toggle stored in localStorage, propagated as data-theme attribute on html element. UNSAID: CSS custom properties (variables) are the most performant theming mechanism — changing one attribute triggers CSS cascade updates without JavaScript re-renders. Tailwind CSS 3.3+ dark mode with `darkMode: 'class'` supports this pattern: add/remove 'dark' class on html element. The React component never needs to know about the current theme — CSS cascade handles it.

React authentication patterns: Auth context provides user state + login/logout actions to the component tree. Protected routes check auth state and redirect to login if unauthenticated. Token storage: httpOnly cookies (not accessible to JavaScript, XSS-safe) vs localStorage (accessible to JavaScript, XSS-vulnerable). UNSAID: storing JWT in localStorage is a security anti-pattern — any XSS vulnerability can steal the token. httpOnly cookies with CSRF protection are the secure alternative. Next.js + NextAuth (Auth.js) handles this correctly: sessions in httpOnly cookies, CSRF tokens managed automatically. The common mistake: building auth from scratch when NextAuth, Clerk, or Auth0 handle all edge cases including refresh token rotation, session invalidation, and OAuth flows.

React code generation with AI tools: Copilot, Cursor, and Claude all handle React component generation well. The optimal prompt structure: describe the component's behavior (not implementation), include TypeScript interface for props, specify which libraries to use (Tailwind, shadcn), and include existing related component as context. UNSAID: AI-generated React code commonly makes these errors: (1) using array index as key, (2) missing cleanup in useEffect, (3) exhaustive-deps violations, (4) using class components instead of functional. Always run eslint + TypeScript after AI generation. The most valuable AI React use case is generating boilerplate (forms, tables, modals) from TypeScript interface definitions — it handles the repetitive wiring that humans find tedious.

React Native and React Web share the component model but NOT the component library. React Native uses View/Text/ScrollView, not div/span/p. Code sharing between web and native requires: React Native Web (renders RN components to DOM), Solito (Next.js + Expo navigation sharing), or explicit platform files (Component.web.tsx vs Component.native.tsx). UNSAID: the common mistake is attempting to share React web components directly in React Native — it fails at runtime because native doesn't have a DOM. Expo Router (file-based routing matching Next.js) is the current best practice for new cross-platform projects. The realism check: true code sharing between web and native is achievable for business logic (hooks, utilities, API calls) but UI components need platform-specific implementations.

React micro-frontends: Module Federation (Webpack 5) allows multiple React apps to share components and state at runtime. Each micro-frontend is independently deployed and can use different React versions (with version compatibility constraints). UNSAID: Module Federation is complex operationally — version mismatches between host and remote React cause runtime errors. The simpler alternative for most teams is monorepo with shared component library (turborepo + npm workspaces), which provides code sharing without runtime complexity. True runtime micro-frontends are appropriate only for large organizations with independent deployment requirements (different teams, different release cycles, different tech stacks).

React Storybook for component development: isolated component development, visual testing, and documentation. Stories define component variants. Chromatic (Storybook's cloud service) provides visual regression testing — screenshot comparison between PRs. Integration with Testing Library via @storybook/testing-library enables interaction testing within stories. UNSAID: Storybook's value is inversely proportional to component coupling — highly coupled components that require deep context/provider setup have expensive story setup. Components designed with Storybook in mind tend to be more composable and prop-driven. Storybook 7+ introduced Component Story Format 3 (CSF3) with auto-title based on file path and simplified args. The play function enables interaction testing (click, type, assert) within Storybook.

React app security hardening checklist: (1) CSP headers preventing inline scripts and unauthorized resource loading, (2) dangerouslySetInnerHTML only with DOMPurify sanitization, (3) JWT in httpOnly cookies (not localStorage), (4) Input validation with Zod on both client and server, (5) CORS configured to only allow your domain, (6) Rate limiting on API endpoints, (7) Dependency audit with `npm audit`, (8) React Query's request deduplication prevents accidental data exposure via timing attacks. UNSAID: most React security issues are in the API layer, not the React layer — but the client can make them worse. Using dangerouslySetInnerHTML with user content is the most common React-specific XSS vector. Sanitize with DOMPurify before any innerHTML usage, always.

React dev environment common setup failures: (1) Vite HMR not working because WSS connection blocked by firewall or proxy, (2) TypeScript paths (@/) resolving in TypeScript but failing at runtime (missing vite.config alias), (3) ESLint React plugin version mismatch with React version, (4) Testing Library queries failing because ARIA roles don't match expected roles. UNSAID: path alias mismatches are the most common 'it works in TypeScript but fails at runtime' error. The fix requires syncing tsconfig.json paths AND vite.config.ts resolve.alias for the same prefix. Runtime module resolution is completely independent of TypeScript resolution — TypeScript uses tsconfig, Vite uses its own resolver.

React's useDebugValue hook adds custom labels to custom hooks in React DevTools. `useDebugValue(value)` shows the value next to the hook name in DevTools. `useDebugValue(value, formatFn)` defers formatting until DevTools is open (avoids expensive formatting in production). UNSAID: useDebugValue is ONLY useful for hooks that appear in multiple components and would otherwise be ambiguous in DevTools. It has no effect in production — DevTools is not installed. The practical use case: a useSubscription hook showing the subscription key, a useAuth hook showing the user ID, a useQuery hook showing the query key. It does not make the value accessible programmatically — it's purely a DevTools display aid.

React Progressive Web App (PWA) setup: Vite PWA plugin (vite-plugin-pwa) wraps Workbox to generate service workers and manifest. Service workers cache assets and API responses for offline functionality. Web App Manifest enables 'Add to Home Screen' on mobile. UNSAID: service workers intercept ALL network requests — incorrect cache strategies can serve stale API responses. Use NetworkFirst or StaleWhileRevalidate for API calls, CacheFirst for static assets. The PWA install prompt is controlled by the browser, not the developer — you can listen for `beforeinstallprompt` to show a custom install button. Push notifications require explicit user permission and a push service (Firebase Cloud Messaging or Web Push Protocol with VAPID keys).

React's decision to NOT include a built-in HTTP client, router, or state management is intentional: it keeps the core small and allows the ecosystem to experiment and converge on best solutions. This led to the flourishing of TanStack Query, React Router, Zustand, and others — libraries that are better than anything React's team would have built while maintaining the core. UNSAID: the flip side is decision fatigue — new React developers face too many choices. The community has converged on a 'React meta-framework' stack: Next.js (routing + SSR), TanStack Query (server state), Zustand (client state), Zod (validation), react-hook-form (forms), Tailwind (styling). This stack is not official but is the de-facto standard for production apps.

React key extraction anti-patterns in dynamic UIs: generating keys from array index is wrong for mutable lists (items added/deleted/reordered). Using Math.random() as key is catastrophically wrong — forces remount on every render, destroying input focus, animation states, and form data. Using content (e.g., the text of a todo item) as key fails when content can change or duplicate. UNSAID: the correct key source is a stable ID from the data — database row IDs, UUIDs, slug fields. If your data doesn't have IDs, that's a backend API design problem. Generate IDs at creation time (nanoid, crypto.randomUUID()) and store them — not at render time. For static non-reordered lists (navigation menus that never change), array index as key is acceptable.

React table with server-side operations: TanStack Table v8 + TanStack Query. Table state (sorting, filtering, pagination) stored in URL params (useSearchParams) for shareable URLs. Each state change triggers TanStack Query refetch with new params. Pattern: URL → parse state → build query key → fetch → render table. UNSAID: storing table state in URL params enables bookmarkable views, browser back/forward navigation, and sharing filtered views — significant UX advantages. The URL serialization/deserialization for complex filter objects requires careful encoding. nuqs library provides type-safe URL state management for Next.js, eliminating manual URLSearchParams manipulation.

React + Redux Toolkit common mistakes: (1) mutating state outside Immer produce (RTK uses Immer internally but only in createSlice reducers), (2) storing non-serializable values in Redux (functions, class instances, Promises) — Redux DevTools serialization fails, (3) using createSelector incorrectly — the last function is the selector, not the first. UNSAID: RTK Query (included in Redux Toolkit) is often the right choice over TanStack Query for teams already using Redux — it provides the same caching benefits without adding another library. The createAsyncThunk pattern for async actions in RTK is verbose compared to TanStack Query but integrates cleanly with Redux DevTools for debugging async flows.

React toast/notification system: react-hot-toast and sonner are the modern choices (2024). Both use a Toaster root component + toast() imperative API. Sonner is shadcn/ui's recommended choice, built by the shadcn author. UNSAID: the imperative API (toast.success('message')) instead of a declarative component API is intentional — toasts are side effects triggered from event handlers, not render logic. The common mistake is creating a custom notification system using Context + useState — this reinvents what react-hot-toast provides (queue management, deduplication, stack positioning, accessibility announcements). Toast announcements must use ARIA live regions for screen reader accessibility — react-hot-toast handles this.

React SSR data leakage: server-rendered React can accidentally leak server-side data between users when module-level variables or singletons hold request-specific state. In Next.js, each request should have its own data — using module-level caches without per-request isolation leaks user A's data to user B. UNSAID: React 18's cache() function creates per-request memoization in Server Components — the correct tool for request-scoped caching. The classic failure pattern: a module-level `let currentUser = null` set during server rendering — concurrent requests corrupt each other's state. Next.js's cookies(), headers(), and database connections are request-scoped by default precisely to prevent this.

React query param management with nuqs (Next.js URL Search Params): type-safe URL state for Next.js. `useQueryState('search', parseAsString.withDefault(''))` reads/writes URL params with type safety. Shallow routing updates URL without triggering full navigation. UNSAID: URL state is the correct place for: search queries, filter state, pagination, sort order, modal visibility (for deep-linkable modals). URL state is NOT appropriate for: sensitive data, large objects, ephemeral UI state (hover, focus). The key principle: URL is global shared state — it should contain state that users want to bookmark, share, or navigate back to. nuqs eliminates the boilerplate of manually encoding/decoding URL params for each filter type.

React map integration: react-leaflet (Leaflet.js wrapper) for open-source maps, @vis.gl/react-google-maps for Google Maps, mapbox-gl with react-map-gl for Mapbox. All require dynamic import with SSR disabled in Next.js (window is not defined on server). UNSAID: Leaflet is the only fully free option — Google Maps and Mapbox have usage-based pricing that can be surprisingly expensive at scale. OpenStreetMap tile servers can be used with Leaflet for small projects but have usage limits. Mapbox GL JS renders maps with WebGL, enabling 3D buildings, custom layers, and GPU-accelerated animations. The 'react-leaflet' SSR failure (window is not defined) is the most common first-time React + map error — always use `dynamic(() => import('./Map'), { ssr: false })` in Next.js.

React Redux connect() vs useSelector/useDispatch: React-Redux v8 hooks are the modern API. The connect() HOC is legacy — verbose, harder to type with TypeScript, and creates unnecessary component nesting. Migrating from connect() to hooks improves code clarity and TypeScript inference. UNSAID: connect() performance optimization via mapStateToProps still requires memoized selectors (reselect/createSelector) — useSelector has the same requirement. The shallow equality comparison of connect()'s mapStateToProps return value is different from useSelector's default reference equality — custom equalityFn in useSelector (like shallowEqual from react-redux) replicates connect()'s behavior. TypeScript typing of connect() with mapDispatchToProps is notoriously complex; useDispatch with typed dispatch is cleaner.

React TypeScript migration strategy from JavaScript: (1) Add TypeScript config with strict:false initially, (2) Rename files one-by-one from .jsx to .tsx, (3) Add 'any' types to silence errors, (4) Gradually replace 'any' with real types, (5) Enable strict mode after all errors resolved. UNSAID: attempting strict:true migration all at once in a large codebase is a common failure — hundreds of errors are overwhelming. The incremental approach with any as scaffolding enables shipping while improving type coverage. The checkJs:true option in tsconfig provides TypeScript checking for remaining .js files without renaming. ts-migrate (Airbnb's tool) automates the initial rename + any insertion step.

React PDF generation: react-pdf (renders React components to PDF using @react-pdf/renderer), jsPDF + html2canvas (screenshots DOM to canvas then saves as PDF). Use cases: invoices, reports, certificates. UNSAID: react-pdf renders to a PDF document model (not DOM) — it has its own styling system (StyleSheet.create), limited font support, and doesn't use HTML/CSS. It's ideal for data-driven PDFs (invoices). html2canvas screenshots are better for WYSIWYG (report exactly as the user sees it on screen) but have quality issues with complex CSS, shadows, and gradients. Server-side PDF generation (Puppeteer headless Chrome, Playwright) produces the highest-quality output — renders actual HTML/CSS — but requires a server and is slower.

React memory leaks from async operations: when an async operation (fetch, setTimeout, setInterval, event listener) updates component state after the component has unmounted, React logs a warning (React 17) or silently ignores it (React 18). Even in React 18 where it's silent, the async operation continues running, wasting resources. Cleanup: AbortController for fetch, clearTimeout/clearInterval for timers, removeEventListener for events, unsubscribe() for observables. UNSAID: the 'Can't perform state update on unmounted component' warning was removed in React 18 because useEffect cleanup handles it properly. But NOT having cleanup is still a resource leak — the async work continues even though nobody cares about the result. The real cost is accumulating event listeners and intervals across route navigations in SPAs.

React form validation strategy: Zod schema validation integrated with React Hook Form via @hookform/resolvers. Schema defined once, used for both client validation and TypeScript types. `z.infer<typeof schema>` extracts TypeScript type from Zod schema — single source of truth. Server-side: same Zod schema validates Server Action inputs. UNSAID: using Zod transforms in form schemas can cause issues — transforming values (string to number) changes the form's value type, which requires `useForm<z.output<typeof schema>>` instead of `z.infer`. The difference between z.input (before transform) and z.output (after transform) is a common TypeScript type error source in RHF+Zod integration. SafeParse (not parse) in Server Actions returns {success, data, error} instead of throwing, enabling clean error return from server actions.

React rendering modes: CSR (Client-Side Rendering) — initial HTML is empty, JS fetches data and renders; SSR (Server-Side Rendering) — HTML generated per-request on server; SSG (Static Site Generation) — HTML generated at build time; ISR (Incremental Static Regeneration, Next.js) — SSG with background revalidation. The choice affects SEO (crawlers see SSR/SSG content immediately), TTFB (SSG fastest, SSR slower, CSR fastest TTFB but empty HTML), and infrastructure cost (SSG is CDN-served, SSR requires server compute). UNSAID: Next.js App Router defaults to SSR for every page — you opt out with 'use client'. The Pages Router defaulted to SSG/CSR. This architectural shift means Next.js App Router apps are fundamentally server-rendered applications with client interactivity layered on top, not SPAs with optional SSR.

React accessibility testing: jest-axe integrates axe-core accessibility checks into Jest/Vitest tests. `expect(await axe(container)).toHaveNoViolations()` automatically checks rendered HTML for WCAG violations. Storybook accessibility addon runs axe in real-time during component development. Manual keyboard testing (Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, Escape, Arrow keys) is required for complex interactions. UNSAID: automated tools catch only ~30-40% of accessibility issues — manual testing is irreplaceable. Screen reader testing with VoiceOver (macOS/iOS) and NVDA (Windows) is the ground truth. The most impactful automated check: jest-axe on every component test ensures structural accessibility. Focus management for modals, dropdown menus, and drawer panels requires manual testing.

React context provider placement: placing providers at the app root is correct for truly global state (theme, auth, i18n). Placing providers too high causes unnecessary re-renders for unrelated components. Placing providers too low can't share state between sibling trees. UNSAID: the over-centralization anti-pattern: putting all providers at the root because it's 'easier' — leads to a 'providers hell' with 10+ nested providers and performance issues from excessive re-renders. The correct approach: colocate providers with the subtree that needs them. A PaymentContext provider should wrap only the checkout flow, not the entire app. This also improves code organization — the boundary is visible in the tree.

React Strict Effects (React.use + Suspense data pattern): reading a resource with `use(resource)` inside a component suspends rendering until the resource resolves. This enables waterfall-free parallel data fetching when resources are created outside the component. Pattern: create promises at the parent level (before rendering children), pass them as props, children call `use(promise)`. UNSAID: `use()` is different from `await` — it doesn't block execution, it suspends the component and React resumes rendering when the promise resolves. The resource (promise) must be created OUTSIDE the component — creating it inside causes a new promise on every render. This pattern eliminates the useEffect + loading state + data state boilerplate for server data fetching in React 19.

React component library creation with Vite library mode: configure `build.lib` in vite.config.ts, set `build.rollupOptions.external` for React and React-DOM (don't bundle them), output both ESM and CJS formats. tsup is an alternative (simpler config, uses esbuild). UNSAID: the critical step that's often forgotten: exporting TypeScript declarations. Set `types` field in package.json pointing to generated .d.ts files. Use `vite-plugin-dts` to generate declarations. Without type exports, consumers get no autocomplete. CSS-in-JS (Tailwind, CSS Modules) in libraries has extra complexity: Tailwind requires consumers to include your library paths in their tailwind.config content array; CSS Modules generates a separate CSS file that consumers must import.

React hydration with third-party scripts: analytics scripts, chat widgets, and ad scripts injected via script tags can interfere with React hydration if they modify the DOM before React hydrates. This causes hydration mismatches and unpredictable behavior. UNSAID: the correct injection point for third-party scripts in Next.js is the Script component with `strategy='lazyOnload'` or `strategy='afterInteractive'` — these ensure React hydrates first. `strategy='beforeInteractive'` blocks hydration and should only be used for critical scripts (consent management). Google Tag Manager async loading is compatible with React hydration. Ad networks that use document.write() are incompatible with modern React — they must inject after hydration via refs.

React concurrent UI patterns: useDeferredValue defers a non-urgent value update, keeping the UI responsive while heavy renders happen in the background. Pattern: `const deferredSearchTerm = useDeferredValue(searchTerm)`. The search input updates immediately; the filtered results re-render in the background without blocking typing. `isPending = deferredSearchTerm !== searchTerm` indicates a pending update — use to show a loading indicator. UNSAID: the correct use case for useDeferredValue is expensive synchronous renders (filtering large lists, computing expensive derived data). It's NOT for network requests (use TanStack Query) or async operations. The key mental model: deferredValue is always one update behind the actual value — the UI shows stale results while computing fresh ones, then switches atomically.

React prop drilling is a symptom, not a disease. The solutions in order of preference: (1) Component composition (render children, slots) — no state needed, (2) Custom hooks — extract logic, components stay slim, (3) Context — for genuinely cross-cutting state, (4) Global state (Zustand) — for app-wide state with many readers/writers. Jumping to global state for 2-level prop drilling is over-engineering. UNSAID: 'prop drilling' that passes 2 levels is not a problem. 4+ levels suggests a component architecture problem — too many layers of container components wrapping presentational ones. The real fix is usually flattening the component tree by lifting rendering responsibility higher, not adding state management.

React with tRPC (TypeScript-safe RPC): define procedures on the server, call them on the client with full type safety and autocomplete. No manual API contract maintenance — TypeScript types flow from server to client automatically. Integration with TanStack Query: tRPC v11 wraps TanStack Query, providing useQuery/useMutation with server procedure types. UNSAID: tRPC is a monorepo solution — server and client must share the same TypeScript project or have the server router type exported to the client package. For separate frontend/backend repos, OpenAPI + Zod (or Orval code generation) is the alternative. tRPC is a significant DX improvement for full-stack TypeScript (Next.js + Node.js) — it eliminates an entire category of runtime bugs caused by frontend-backend type drift.

React DevTools extension does not show Server Components state (they have no state). Server Components appear in the tree as their name but without the hooks panel. Attempting to debug Server Component data by looking at DevTools state is a failure mode — the data is passed as props to Client Components, visible in the Client Component's props panel.
