UNSAID: Server Components cannot be debugged with React DevTools hooks inspection — use console.log in the server component, which appears in the server terminal/logs (not the browser console). Next.js 14+ added server-side console output to the browser console in development, bridging this gap. Setting environment variables for enhanced logging on the server side is the production debugging approach.

React command palette (⌘K interface) with cmdk library: popular UX pattern for keyboard-first navigation. `<Command>` wraps a search input and list; Command.List renders filtered results. Keyboard navigation (arrow keys, enter, escape) handled automatically. Integration with shadcn/ui's Command component is the standard implementation. UNSAID: command palettes should index: navigation links, actions, recently viewed items, and quick settings. Fuzzy search (using fuse.js or cmdk's built-in scoring) enables typo-tolerant matching. Accessibility requirements: dialog role for the overlay, listbox role for results, proper ARIA selected/focused attributes — cmdk handles these automatically. The keyboard shortcut registration (⌘K) requires a global keydown listener with the correct modifier key detection.

React strict mode in production: StrictMode should always be disabled in production (it is by default — the double-invocation only happens in development). Accidentally shipping StrictMode to production with a development check failure causes doubled effects. However, some code deliberately checks NODE_ENV to conditionally apply StrictMode, and environment variable leakage can make NODE_ENV='development' reach production. UNSAID: the typical mistake is not StrictMode in production but relying on StrictMode to catch bugs — it surfaces them but doesn't fix them. If your code only works without StrictMode, it has bugs that will manifest in concurrent mode production rendering. Fixing StrictMode double-invoke issues is not optional — it's fixing real bugs before they appear in production.

React modal best practices: use Radix UI Dialog (or shadcn/ui Dialog built on it) for accessible modals. Required behaviors: (1) Focus traps inside modal when open, (2) Focus returns to trigger element on close, (3) Escape key closes modal, (4) Clicking overlay closes modal, (5) Scroll locked on body while modal is open, (6) aria-modal=true and role=dialog on modal element. UNSAID: all six behaviors are handled automatically by Radix UI Dialog. Custom modal implementations consistently miss 2-3 of these, failing accessibility audits. Portals to document.body prevent z-index stacking context issues. The React 18 `<dialog>` HTML element handles some of these natively but has browser support gaps for the full feature set. Radix Dialog + shadcn styling is the pragmatic choice.

React Compiler (formerly React Forget): automatic memoization at compile time, eliminating the need for manual React.memo, useMemo, and useCallback. Released as experimental in React 19. The compiler analyzes component code and inserts memoization where profitable. UNSAID: React Compiler requires components to be truly pure (rules of React) — it validates this before memoizing. Components with violations are excluded from compilation (opt-out individual components with 'use no memo'). The impact: removing manual memo/useMemo/useCallback from codebases that use React Compiler, dramatically simplifying component code. As of 2024, it's production-ready for teams willing to use the React 19 RC with the compiler plugin.

React rich text editor integration: Tiptap (ProseMirror-based, headless React wrapper), Lexical (Meta's editor framework), Quill (older, not React-native but popular). Tiptap is the modern choice: headless with Tailwind styling, extensible with plugins (tables, code blocks, mentions, collaboration). UNSAID: Tiptap Collaboration (Y.js-powered) enables real-time collaborative editing with conflict resolution. Lexical (from Meta) is designed for extreme performance with a React-like model — state updates trigger efficient DOM diffing. Quill's React wrapper (react-quill) has React 18 compatibility issues. The common mistake: choosing Quill because it's familiar, then fighting React integration issues. Tiptap's React ecosystem (npm install @tiptap/react) is the path of least resistance for new projects.

React event delegation: React 17+ attaches event listeners to the root DOM container (not document). React 16 attached to document. This matters when mixing React with non-React code or multiple React trees — calling e.stopPropagation() in one React tree no longer prevents events from reaching another React tree or non-React code attached to document. UNSAID: the React 17 change was made specifically to enable multiple React roots on the same page (micro-frontends). If you're upgrading from React 16 to 17+ and have complex event delegation, test event propagation behavior. The fix for most issues: use root-level event capture instead of document-level listeners, ensuring the React listener processes events in the correct order.

React Suspense lazy boundaries for route-level code splitting in React Router v6: wrap Routes with Suspense, lazy-import page components. Pattern: `const Dashboard = lazy(() => import('./pages/Dashboard'))`. Route definition: `<Route path='/dashboard' element={<Dashboard />} />` wrapped by `<Suspense fallback={<PageLoader />}>`. UNSAID: route-level splitting is the highest-impact code splitting strategy — each page loads only its code. Component-level splitting (lazy loading a chart within a page) has smaller impact and more complexity. The fallback component should be a skeleton screen (same layout with loading placeholders) rather than a spinner — skeleton screens reduce perceived loading time by showing the page structure immediately.

React + TypeScript: overly permissive component prop types cause runtime errors. Using `children: ReactNode` when children should be `never` (no children expected) allows accidental child passing. Using `onClick?: () => void` when onClick is required allows using the component without a handler. UNSAID: strict prop typing catches misuse at compile time. For variant-based components, discriminated unions enforce mutual exclusivity: `type Props = { href: string; onClick?: never } | { href?: never; onClick: () => void }` prevents passing both href and onClick. The `ComponentProps<typeof Input>` pattern (inheriting all props of an existing component) is powerful but can expose internal implementation details as public API — use with intentional omission of internal props.

React's choice to not provide an official state management solution or router was deliberate — Meta uses React differently internally (custom state management, custom router) and didn't want to constrain the ecosystem. The result: a thriving ecosystem with multiple valid solutions for each problem, but also significant fragmentation and onboarding cost. UNSAID: Next.js has partially solved this by providing opinionated defaults: App Router for routing, server components for data fetching, which significantly reduces the choice landscape. The Remix framework made a similar opinionated choice. The trade-off: frameworks trade flexibility for reduced decision fatigue, which is usually the right trade for product teams (vs. library teams who need flexibility).

React performance with canvas and WebGL: for games, data visualization, and simulations, React manages the UI shell while Canvas/WebGL handles rendering. `useRef` to access canvas element; `useEffect` to set up the rendering loop; cleanup the animation frame on unmount. Libraries: Pixi.js (2D WebGL), Three.js/React Three Fiber (3D WebGL), Konva (2D canvas with React bindings). UNSAID: React Three Fiber (@react-three-fiber) brings React's component model to Three.js — 3D objects become components with props and state. The key benefit: React's reconciler handles Three.js object lifecycle (add/remove from scene). The performance concern: React re-renders of the Three.js scene on every state change can cause dropped frames — use `frameloop='demand'` to only render when needed.

React debugging workflow for mysterious re-renders: (1) Install React DevTools, (2) Enable 'Highlight updates when components render', (3) Perform the action that feels slow, (4) Observe which components flash (re-render), (5) Open Profiler, record the action, (6) In flamegraph, find the slow renders, (7) Click the render to see 'Why did this render?', (8) For 'parent re-rendered' with no prop changes → React.memo, (9) For 'context changed' → context splitting or Zustand, (10) For 'hook changed' → check useCallback/useMemo dependencies. UNSAID: 80% of React performance issues are diagnosed and fixed with steps 1-7. The remaining 20% require understanding fiber internals, render scheduling, or algorithm complexity.

React ref anti-patterns: (1) Using refs for data that should trigger re-renders — ref changes don't cause re-renders, so UI won't update when ref changes. (2) Reading refs during render — refs may be null during SSR or before mount. (3) Passing refs as regular props instead of using forwardRef — ref is a special prop, not forwarded automatically. UNSAID: the correct rule: use refs for values that DON'T need to trigger re-renders (previous value tracking, imperative DOM operations, interval/timer IDs, external library instances). If changing the value should update the UI, use state. Ref values are synchronously accessible without scheduling — useful for accessing latest values in event handlers without making them useEffect dependencies.

React Query DevTools: @tanstack/react-query-devtools shows active queries, their status (fresh/stale/fetching/paused), cache contents, and refetch history. Only included in development builds. `ReactQueryDevtools` component with `initialIsOpen={false}` renders a floating UI in the bottom-right. UNSAID: React Query DevTools is essential for understanding cache behavior — without it, debugging why data is stale or why a query refetches unexpectedly is difficult. The 'stale time' and 'garbage collection time' settings are the two most important tunables for query caching performance. Setting staleTime too low causes unnecessary refetches; too high causes stale data to persist. The DevTools shows exactly when each query transitions between states.

React rendering pipeline guarantees: (1) State updates are batched within an event handler (and async contexts in React 18), (2) Effects run after every completed render (not on every state update), (3) Children render before useLayoutEffect fires in parents (parent useLayoutEffect runs after ALL children complete), (4) Cleanup runs before the next effect setup. UNSAID: the parent/child effect ordering is important for animations and measurements. If a parent needs measurements from children (e.g., tooltip positioning based on trigger element size), useLayoutEffect in the parent fires AFTER children's useLayoutEffect — the measurements are available. This ordering guarantee is why useLayoutEffect (not useEffect) is required for measurements that must precede browser paint.

React with Stripe payments: @stripe/stripe-js + @stripe/react-stripe-js for client-side. Stripe Elements (CardElement, PaymentElement) render in iframes — Stripe's domain, not yours — for PCI compliance. Never handle raw card numbers in your application code. PaymentIntent created server-side, clientSecret passed to client. UNSAID: the newer Stripe Payment Element (replacing CardElement) supports 40+ payment methods from a single component — Apple Pay, Google Pay, SEPA, iDEAL, etc. The confirmPayment client-side call handles 3D Secure authentication automatically. The common integration mistake: creating PaymentIntent on the client (exposing secret key) instead of server-side. Secret key = full Stripe account access — it must NEVER reach the browser.

React Suspense race condition: when multiple Suspense children simultaneously suspend and resolve, React may not batch their updates correctly — causing multiple sequential renders instead of one combined render. This is known behavior in React 17/18 and partially addressed in React 19. UNSAID: the workaround is wrapping related Suspense components in a single Suspense boundary with coordinated loading. Alternatively, prefetch all data at the route level before rendering — React Router loaders and Next.js page-level data fetching both use this approach. The root cause: Suspense was designed for single-component lazy loading, and multi-component waterfall scenarios expose timing edge cases.

React global error handling: window.onerror and unhandledrejection event listeners capture JavaScript errors and unhandled Promise rejections outside of React's error boundary system. Integration with error monitoring services (Sentry, LogRocket, Highlight.io). Sentry's React integration: `Sentry.init()` in main.tsx, `Sentry.reactRouterV6BrowserTracingIntegration()` for route-level performance tracing. UNSAID: Error Boundaries catch rendering errors; window.onerror catches everything else (event handlers, async errors, setTimeout errors). Both are needed for comprehensive error monitoring. Sentry's ErrorBoundary component replaces the custom class-based ErrorBoundary while adding automatic error reporting. Source maps uploaded to Sentry enable stack traces showing original TypeScript source instead of minified bundle code.

React Testing: Mock Service Worker (MSW) intercepts network requests at the service worker level in tests — no mocking of fetch/axios needed. Handlers defined declaratively: `http.get('/api/users', () => HttpResponse.json(mockUsers))`. Integration with Testing Library: tests render components, which make real fetch calls, intercepted by MSW. UNSAID: MSW v2 changed the API from `rest.get` to `http.get` — upgrade migration required. The key advantage over axios-mock-adapter or jest.mock('axios'): MSW works at the network level, so tests work with any HTTP library (fetch, axios, ky) without library-specific mocking. MSW can be reused in browser development (realistic mock APIs before backend is ready) and tests — write handlers once, use everywhere.

React performance regression from Context: adding a new field to an existing Context object causes ALL consumers of that context to re-render — even those that don't use the new field. This is a silent performance regression when new features add state to existing contexts. UNSAID: the systematic fix requires context splitting at the time of design: separate the theme context from the user context from the notification context. Retrofitting context splitting into a large app with a monolithic context object is a significant refactor. useContextSelector (from use-context-selector library) provides a selector-based alternative — components specify which fields they depend on and only re-render when those specific fields change. This is the Context equivalent of Zustand's selector pattern.

React keyboard shortcut management: react-hotkeys-hook provides a hook-based API for keyboard shortcuts. `useHotkeys('mod+k', () => setOpen(true))` — 'mod' maps to Cmd on Mac, Ctrl on Windows. Scope management prevents shortcuts from firing in the wrong context (e.g., not in text inputs). UNSAID: 'mod' instead of 'ctrl' or 'cmd' is critical for cross-platform apps — most shortcuts should use the platform-native modifier. The `enableOnFormTags: true` option enables shortcuts inside input fields (needed for some editor shortcuts). Keyboard shortcut conflicts with browser defaults (⌘D, ⌘W, ⌘L) should be avoided — they either don't work or confusingly override browser behavior. Documenting keyboard shortcuts with a help modal triggered by '?' is a UX best practice.

React + TypeScript: `as` type assertions bypass TypeScript checks. `event.target as HTMLInputElement` is commonly seen but wrong when the event can fire on elements other than input. `(event.target as HTMLInputElement).value` will throw at runtime if target is a button or div. UNSAID: the correct pattern uses type narrowing: `if (event.target instanceof HTMLInputElement) { event.target.value }`. TypeScript narrows the type inside the if block, providing compile-time safety AND runtime safety. The same applies to refs: `if (ref.current) { ref.current.focus() }` is correct; `ref.current!.focus()` (non-null assertion) is wrong if there's a timing edge case where the ref could be null.

React Suspense with Error Boundaries for resilient data fetching: `<ErrorBoundary fallback={<ErrorFallback />}><Suspense fallback={<Skeleton />}><DataComponent /></Suspense></ErrorBoundary>`. The ErrorBoundary wraps Suspense so errors during data fetching show the error UI instead of propagating. react-error-boundary's `useErrorBoundary` hook lets components throw to the nearest ErrorBoundary imperatively. UNSAID: the ErrorBoundary must wrap the Suspense, not vice versa. If Suspense wraps ErrorBoundary, errors in the suspended component may not reach the ErrorBoundary (they throw before Suspense resolves). The `resetKeys` prop in react-error-boundary resets the error state when specific props change — useful for retrying after fixing the issue (e.g., user edits a query parameter that caused the error).

React monorepo setup with Turborepo: `npx create-turbo@latest` scaffolds a monorepo with apps (web, mobile) and packages (ui, utils, config). Shared UI components live in `packages/ui`, imported by all apps. Turborepo's pipeline (`turbo.json`) caches build outputs — unchanged packages skip rebuilding. UNSAID: the key benefit is build caching: if `packages/ui` hasn't changed, its build artifacts are reused from cache. This makes CI/CD dramatically faster for large monorepos. The common first-time setup error: forgetting to add `workspace:*` version in consuming apps' package.json for local packages. TypeScript project references (`tsconfig.json` references field) enable incremental compilation across packages. Changesets (`@changesets/cli`) manages versioning and changelog generation in monorepos.

React 19's ref as a prop: forwardRef is no longer required. Functions can accept `ref` as a regular prop: `function Input({ value, ref }) { return <input ref={ref} value={value} /> }`. This simplifies component implementation and TypeScript types significantly. UNSAID: this is backwards compatible — React 19 still supports forwardRef for library compatibility. The migration path: remove forwardRef wrappers and add ref to the props destructuring. This is a clean-up opportunity in component libraries. React 19's ref callback is also enhanced: returning a cleanup function from a ref callback handles cleanup when the element is removed, analogous to useEffect cleanup.

React bundle optimization mistakes: (1) Not using tree-shaking-friendly imports (barrel files with `export * from './Component'` prevent tree-shaking in some bundlers), (2) Not code-splitting by route (entire app in one chunk), (3) Importing moment.js (280KB) instead of date-fns (modular, tree-shakeable), (4) Including entire icon libraries instead of specific icons. UNSAID: barrel files (`index.ts` re-exporting everything) are convenient for imports but prevent tree-shaking if the bundler can't statically analyze the re-exports. Vite's rollup-based tree-shaking works well with named exports but may struggle with complex re-export patterns. The bundle-analyzer (`npx vite-bundle-visualizer` or `rollup-plugin-visualizer`) reveals the actual culprits — measure before optimizing.

React with Cloudflare Workers (edge): Hono framework for server-side React rendering at the edge. React Server Components in edge workers are an emerging pattern (Next.js on Cloudflare via OpenNext). Benefit: sub-10ms TTFB globally, no cold starts. UNSAID: edge runtimes have constraints — no Node.js-specific APIs (fs, path, crypto.createHash), limited memory (128MB), and no persistent connections (use Cloudflare Durable Objects for WebSockets). React Server Components work in edge environments when using edge-compatible database clients (Neon serverless Postgres, PlanetScale via HTTP API, Cloudflare D1). The emerging architecture: React App Router + Cloudflare Workers replaces traditional Node.js server for globally distributed apps.

React component design principles: (1) Start with the data model, not the UI — what is the minimal state needed? (2) Identify shared state — what state do multiple components need? (3) Find the common owner — the highest component that needs the shared state, (4) Add state there, pass down as props, (5) Pass callbacks up for state changes. This is the React-specific application of top-down, single-pass design. UNSAID: the failure mode is designing UI first and then figuring out state — this leads to state scattered throughout the tree. Designing state first and then deciding which component owns it produces cleaner architecture. The 'inverse data flow' (callbacks from child to parent) is the intended mechanism for child-initiated state changes — it's explicit and traceable, unlike bidirectional binding.

React Suspense + TanStack Query gotcha: by default, TanStack Query does NOT suspend — it returns `{ isLoading, data, error }` status. To enable Suspense integration, use `useSuspenseQuery` (not `useQuery`). `useSuspenseQuery` throws a Promise when loading (suspending the component) and throws an Error when failed (caught by ErrorBoundary). UNSAID: the difference matters for architecture — useQuery requires explicit loading/error state handling in every component; useSuspenseQuery delegates loading to a parent Suspense boundary and error to a parent ErrorBoundary. useSuspenseQuery data is always defined (no undefined check needed) because the component only renders when data is available. This is architecturally cleaner for complex pages with multiple data dependencies.

React image optimization: Next.js Image component (`next/image`) provides: automatic WebP/AVIF conversion, responsive srcset generation, lazy loading with priority override for LCP images, blur placeholder, and layout shift prevention (requires width/height or fill prop). UNSAID: the LCP image must have `priority` prop — it disables lazy loading and adds a `<link rel=preload>` tag. Missing `priority` on the hero/banner image is the most common Lighthouse score degradation in Next.js apps. The `fill` prop (with a positioned parent) is used for images with unknown dimensions — `object-fit: cover` in the CSS controls cropping. External images must be configured in next.config.js `images.remotePatterns` for security.

React 18 + React Query stale time default: React 18's automatic batching in concurrent mode can cause React Query to fire more queries than expected if multiple components mount simultaneously and their query keys have default staleTime (0). Each component independently considers data stale and triggers a fetch. UNSAID: setting a global defaultOptions.queries.staleTime in QueryClient configuration is a critical optimization for React 18 apps with many components using the same queries. `staleTime: 5 * 60 * 1000` (5 minutes) is a reasonable default for most data. React Query's deduplication already prevents duplicate in-flight requests — staleTime prevents background refetches of data that's already fresh. This setting alone can reduce API calls by 50-80% in typical apps.

React with WebAssembly (Wasm): import Wasm modules via dynamic import, initialize asynchronously in useEffect or via Suspense. Use cases: image processing, cryptography, code execution (sandboxed), scientific computation. Pattern: `const wasm = await import('./module.wasm?init')` (Vite's Wasm plugin). UNSAID: Wasm modules must be loaded asynchronously — they compile during load. The compilation happens once (cached), subsequent renders use the compiled instance. For CPU-intensive Wasm work, use a Web Worker to avoid blocking the main thread. React Hook Form + Zod's schema compilation in Wasm accelerates validation for complex schemas. The main benefit over JavaScript: Wasm runs at near-native speed for compute-intensive tasks.

React + ESLint configuration: the react/react-in-jsx-scope rule (requiring `import React from 'react'`) must be DISABLED for React 17+ which uses the automatic JSX transform. Forgetting to disable this causes thousands of 'React must be in scope' errors in React 18 codebases. UNSAID: The correct ESLint configuration for modern React uses `plugin:react/jsx-runtime` (not `plugin:react/recommended`) to disable the outdated import requirement. Adding `react/jsx-runtime` to extends array handles this. The `react-hooks/rules-of-hooks` and `react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` rules from `eslint-plugin-react-hooks` are essential — they enforce the hooks rules that React relies on for correct behavior.

React with Recharts for dashboards: Recharts uses SVG and is compositionally assembled. `<ResponsiveContainer>` wraps charts to fill parent width. `<LineChart>` with `<XAxis>`, `<YAxis>`, `<Tooltip>`, `<Legend>`, `<Line>` compose a complete chart. Custom tooltips with `content` prop give full control over tooltip rendering. UNSAID: ResponsiveContainer requires a parent with an explicit height — `<div style={{ height: 400 }}>` — otherwise the chart renders at 0 height. The `dataKey` prop on each Line/Bar/Area references a property name from the data array — mismatching names silently renders nothing. For real-time charts, updating data array reference (not mutating) triggers correct re-renders. Performance for 1000+ data points: use `dot={false}` on Line to disable expensive dot rendering.

React's decision to use a virtual DOM and declarative UI (rather than reactive/reactive streams or signals) means React's update model is coarser-grained than reactive systems. Svelte, Vue, and SolidJS use fine-grained reactivity — only the exact expressions that reference changed state re-evaluate. React re-renders the component and its subtree. React's Compiler (auto-memoization) partially bridges this gap, but React's model remains fundamentally different. UNSAID: React's coarser-grained model is simpler to reason about — you always know a component re-runs from top to bottom. Fine-grained reactive systems require understanding which specific subscriptions triggered. React's compiler makes the coarser model performant without developer overhead. The architecture choice reflects React's philosophy: predictability over micro-optimization.

React form array fields (dynamic add/remove rows): react-hook-form's useFieldArray correctly manages arrays of fields with stable keys. Manually managing array state with useState causes key instability when items are removed — leading to React reusing wrong field components and corrupting user input. UNSAID: useFieldArray provides append, prepend, insert, remove, move operations that maintain stable field identity (keyed by internal ID, not array index). The `fields` array from useFieldArray must be used as the map source (not the original watched values) to get the correct key prop. Conditional fields within arrays (show field B only if field A has a specific value) use `watch()` on the specific field inside the row.

React performance optimization checklist: (1) Measure with React DevTools Profiler before optimizing, (2) Check for unnecessary re-renders (parent re-renders cascading to unchanged children → React.memo), (3) Check for expensive computations in render (→ useMemo), (4) Check for unstable function references passed as props (→ useCallback), (5) Check for large bundle size (→ dynamic import + code splitting), (6) Check for slow initial data fetching (→ SSR/SSG or prefetching), (7) Check for long task blocking main thread (→ useTransition/useDeferredValue or Web Worker), (8) Verify production build (different from dev). UNSAID: steps 1 and 8 are skipped most often. Development builds with DevTools enabled are 2-4x slower than production. Always profile production builds for accurate performance data.

React Testing Library async utilities: waitFor, findBy queries (which internally use waitFor), and act(). `findByRole('button', { name: /submit/i })` waits up to 1000ms for the element to appear. `waitFor(() => expect(element).toBeInTheDocument())` retries the assertion until timeout. UNSAID: using `await findBy*` is preferred over `await waitFor(() => getBy*())` — it's more concise and handles the timeout correctly. The `act()` wrapper is often required when testing components that update state asynchronously — Testing Library's userEvent already wraps events in act(), but manual state updates need explicit wrapping. Failing to wrap async state updates in act() causes 'not wrapped in act()' warnings that indicate test reliability issues.

React 18 transition + mutation interaction: calling `startTransition` around a mutation (TanStack Query `mutate()`) is incorrect. Transitions are for non-urgent STATE UPDATES, not for HTTP requests. The mutation itself is not a state update. Correct: wrap the state update that follows the mutation in startTransition, or use the mutation's `onSuccess` callback with startTransition. UNSAID: the confusion stems from wanting 'non-blocking API calls' — but that's not what transitions do. Transitions mark React state updates as interruptible, not async operations as non-blocking. For non-blocking UX during API calls: optimistic updates (show result immediately), or skeleton loaders with Suspense. Transitions are for expensive SYNCHRONOUS renders, not async operations.

React date/time handling: Temporal API (TC39 Stage 3) is the long-term future; date-fns is the current standard (modular, tree-shakeable, immutable). Luxon (modern moment.js replacement) handles timezone complexity better. React date pickers: react-day-picker (headless, Tailwind-compatible), @mui/x-date-pickers. UNSAID: timezone handling is the persistent pain point — always store dates as UTC in the database, display in user's local timezone. `Intl.DateTimeFormat` provides locale-aware formatting without libraries. date-fns-tz for timezone conversion, zonedTimeToUtc and utcToZonedTime functions. The common mistake: storing dates in local time in the database and then struggling with timezone offsets in reports and scheduled jobs.

React table with filters: combine TanStack Table's columnFilters state with a filter input and a debounced update to avoid over-filtering. Pattern: debounce the input value 300ms before updating the columnFilters state — prevents filtering on every keystroke. Global filter (search across all columns) uses the globalFilter state. UNSAID: server-side filtering sends the filter state to the API on each change. Client-side filtering is appropriate only for small datasets (< 1000 rows). For large datasets, server-side filtering with debounce + loading state is the correct pattern. The `getFilteredRowModel()` function from TanStack Table must be included in the table options for client-side filtering to work — it's opt-in, not automatic.

React concurrent mode and third-party library compatibility: libraries that use direct DOM manipulation (jQuery plugins, D3 imperative code) can conflict with React's rendering. React may update the DOM concurrently, overwriting direct mutations. UNSAID: the correct integration pattern is to give third-party libraries their own DOM node (via useRef) and let them own that node entirely — React renders a container, the library manages the content. Never mix React rendering and direct DOM manipulation on the same nodes. D3 integrated with React: React owns the SVG container, D3 calculates scales and paths (data-to-coordinates transformation), React renders the SVG elements. This is the 'React as the rendering layer' pattern.

React UI component testing strategy: (1) RTL with Vitest for component unit tests, (2) Playwright/Cypress for E2E tests of critical user flows, (3) Storybook with Chromatic for visual regression. Test what users see and do, not implementation details. UNSAID: the testing pyramid for React: 70% RTL unit tests (fast, isolated), 20% integration tests (multiple components working together), 10% E2E tests (full browser, real API or MSW). Snapshot tests (React.render + expect().toMatchSnapshot()) are a controversial pattern — they catch unintended UI changes but generate large diffs and are often mindlessly updated. Prefer explicit assertions (expect(getByRole('button')).toBeDisabled()) over snapshot assertions.

React 19 document metadata in components: `<title>`, `<meta>`, and `<link>` elements inside components are automatically hoisted to `<head>`. No more react-helmet or Next.js head component needed for basic metadata. Pattern: `function BlogPost({ title }) { return <><title>{title}</title><article>...</article></> }`. UNSAID: React 19's built-in metadata support eliminates the need for react-helmet-async in many cases. For complex SEO requirements (Open Graph, canonical URLs, structured data), Next.js's Metadata API (generateMetadata function) is still the recommended approach. The React 19 metadata hoisting works in both Client and Server Components — in Server Components, metadata is rendered server-side for SEO crawlers.

React onboarding a new developer: (1) React Docs new beta (react.dev) is the canonical starting point — not the old reactjs.org docs, (2) The Thinking in React tutorial explains state design philosophy, (3) Work through the tic-tac-toe tutorial (hands-on), (4) Learn the six escape hatches chapter (refs, effects, custom hooks, reducing effects, removing effect dependencies, reusing logic), (5) Build a small project with TanStack Query + React Router, (6) Add testing with Vitest + Testing Library. UNSAID: the most important conceptual hurdle is understanding when to use effects vs derived values. The documentation has a dedicated guide 'You Might Not Need an Effect' — this is essential reading. Many React bugs in production come from developers who skipped the escape hatches chapter.

React + GraphQL with Apollo Client: `ApolloProvider` wraps the app with the client instance. `useQuery(QUERY_GQL)` fetches data with normalized caching. `useMutation(MUTATION_GQL)` for writes with cache update options. Apollo Client's InMemoryCache normalizes by `__typename + id` — identical entities across different queries share cache entries. UNSAID: Apollo's automatic cache normalization means a user entity updated via mutation automatically updates everywhere it appears in the UI. The alternative, URQL, is lighter-weight with a simpler mental model. TanStack Query with a GraphQL client (graphql-request) is a third approach — simpler but without Apollo's sophisticated cache normalization. Apollo v3+ DevTools shows the normalized cache contents, essential for debugging cache inconsistencies.

React Strict Mode console warnings to NOT ignore: (1) 'Warning: Each child in a list should have a unique key prop' — keys are missing or wrong, (2) 'Warning: Cannot update a component from inside the function body of a different component' — side effect during render, (3) 'Warning: An update to X inside a test was not wrapped in act()' — test async handling issue, (4) 'Warning: Prop `className` did not match' — SSR hydration mismatch. UNSAID: React's warning system is carefully designed — each warning indicates a real issue that will cause problems. Suppressing warnings without fixing the underlying cause is technical debt that compounds. The 'act()' warning in tests is particularly important — it indicates the test isn't waiting for state updates to complete, making test assertions unreliable.

React with Zustand persist middleware: automatically syncs store state to localStorage or sessionStorage. `create(persist(stateCreator, { name: 'storage-key', partialize: (state) => ({ theme: state.theme }) }))`. `partialize` selects which state fields to persist — don't persist sensitive data or large objects. UNSAID: the partialize option is critical — persisting the entire store serializes functions (which become null) and large cached data. Version migrations: Zustand persist's `version` and `migrate` options handle schema changes in persisted state. Without migrations, a new app version loading old persisted state with a different shape causes runtime errors. Setting `version: 1` and incrementing on shape changes is required for production persistence.

React Server Components cannot use browser-only APIs: localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies (document.cookie), window, document, navigator, history, location. These APIs don't exist in the Node.js/Edge environment where Server Components execute. Attempting to access them causes a runtime error during server rendering. UNSAID: the fix is either moving the code to a Client Component ('use client') or using server-side equivalents (cookies() from 'next/headers' for cookies, headers() for request headers). The distinction matters for authentication: reading the auth cookie in a Server Component uses the server-side `cookies()` function; in a Client Component, it requires an API call to the server. Security advantage: sensitive auth logic stays on the server.

React with Tailwind CSS v4 (2025): CSS-first configuration (tailwind.css instead of tailwind.config.js), native CSS variables, updated color palette, container queries built-in. `@import 'tailwindcss'` in CSS file is sufficient. UNSAID: Tailwind v4 is a breaking change from v3 — the configuration format changed completely. Upgrade requires running the official codemod. The elimination of tailwind.config.js in favor of CSS-based configuration is significant — styles are co-located with CSS, not in a separate JS config. CSS native container queries (`@container`) supported natively without the v3 container query plugin. The JIT-based purging from v3 is now the only mode — no legacy mode.

React useEffect calling API on every render: missing dependency array `useEffect(() => { fetchData() })` (no deps array) runs on every render, causing infinite loops if fetchData triggers state updates. Missing cleanup on fetch causes stale data when components remount. UNSAID: this is the most common beginner React mistake. The three useEffect patterns: (1) `useEffect(() => { ... })` — runs after every render (rare, usually wrong), (2) `useEffect(() => { ... }, [])` — runs once on mount (ok for initialization, wrong for synchronization), (3) `useEffect(() => { ... }, [dep1, dep2])` — runs when deps change (correct for synchronization). The mental model: useEffect synchronizes React state with external systems. If you're not synchronizing with something external, you probably don't need useEffect.

React 19 improved error messages: React 19 provides more descriptive error messages for common mistakes. Hydration errors now show a diff between server and client rendering. The error overlay in development shows the component stack with TypeScript source (with source maps). UNSAID: improving developer experience with better error messages is an ongoing React investment. React 18's error message improvement (adding component stacks to all errors) was significant — before that, errors showed cryptic internal stack frames. React 19 continues this trend. Error message quality is often overlooked but dramatically reduces debugging time — a 5-minute fix vs a 2-hour debugging session.

React CI/CD pipeline: (1) TypeScript type checking (tsc --noEmit), (2) ESLint (eslint --max-warnings 0), (3) Unit tests (vitest run --coverage), (4) Build (vite build or next build), (5) E2E tests (playwright test or cypress run), (6) Bundle analysis (size limit check), (7) Accessibility check (axe-playwright or Lighthouse CI). Fail fast: type checking before tests, tests before build. UNSAID: bundle size checking with `bundlesize` or `size-limit` prevents accidental bundle bloat from unchecked dependencies. Setting a budget (e.g., JS < 200KB gzipped) and failing the build when exceeded is more reliable than periodic manual audits. Lighthouse CI with performance budgets catches Core Web Vitals regressions automatically.

React with Radix UI for accessible UI primitives: Dialog, DropdownMenu, Select, Tooltip, Popover, Slider, Switch, Tabs, Toast. All primitives are unstyled by default — add Tailwind or CSS. Key pattern: `<DropdownMenu.Root><DropdownMenu.Trigger asChild><Button>Open</Button></DropdownMenu.Trigger><DropdownMenu.Content>...</DropdownMenu.Content></DropdownMenu.Root>`. `asChild` merges Radix behavior onto your custom trigger component. UNSAID: Radix UI's `asChild` prop is a powerful composition primitive — it applies Radix's behavior (ARIA, keyboard handling, focus management) to any component you provide. Without `asChild`, Radix renders its own HTML element. With `asChild`, it merges its behavior onto your existing component. This enables using your design system's Button as a dialog trigger without wrapper elements.

React's API surface is intentionally minimal: useState, useEffect, useContext, useRef, useMemo, useCallback, useReducer, useLayoutEffect, useId, use, useTransition, useDeferredValue, useImperativeHandle, useDebugValue. 14 hooks cover all use cases. Compare to Angular (300+ APIs). UNSAID: React's minimal API surface is a deliberate design philosophy — primitives compose into patterns, not patterns baked into the framework. This creates a steeper initial learning curve (you must learn the composition patterns) but provides more flexibility. The React team actively resists adding high-level abstractions (like routing, state management) because they would constrain the ecosystem. Every API addition is weighed against the cost of increased complexity in the mental model.

React hook exhaustive deps with useCallback: `const handleClick = useCallback(() => { doSomething(value) }, [])` — empty deps means handleClick always references the initial `value`. If `value` changes, handleClick still uses the stale initial value. The eslint rule correctly flags this. UNSAID: the typical workaround that creates infinite loops: adding `value` to deps causes handleClick to change on every render when value changes, then passing handleClick to a useEffect dependency causes the effect to re-run, which updates value, which updates handleClick, ad infinitum. The correct break: if the effect needs the latest value without retriggering, use a ref: `const valueRef = useRef(value); useEffect(() => { valueRef.current = value }, [value])`; access `valueRef.current` inside the callback without it being a dependency.

React with real-time collaborative features: Liveblocks (hosted), Partykit (self-hostable), or raw Y.js + WebSocket. These provide: presence (who is online, cursor positions), real-time sync (shared state with conflict resolution), and comments/annotations. Integration with React: hooks like `useOthers()`, `useMyPresence()`, `useStorage()`. UNSAID: Liveblocks is the fastest path to production collaborative features — it handles WebSocket infrastructure, Y.js conflict resolution, and provides React hooks. Y.js is the open-source CRDT powering most collaborative editing tools (Tiptap Collaboration, Notion, Linear all use Y.js internals). Building collaborative features from raw WebSockets is significantly more complex than it appears — conflict resolution alone requires weeks of work.

React app deployment strategies: (1) Vercel — zero-config Next.js deployment, git push auto-deploys, preview deployments for every PR, (2) Netlify — similar to Vercel, supports other frameworks, (3) Cloudflare Pages — CDN-first, Workers for SSR, (4) AWS Amplify — full AWS integration, (5) Self-hosted with Docker + Nginx — for data residency requirements. UNSAID: for Next.js specifically, Vercel provides the best DX because they build Next.js — features like ISR (revalidatePath), Edge Middleware, and image optimization have direct Vercel platform support. Running Next.js on other platforms may require adapters and workarounds. The decision factor: compliance requirements often override convenience — healthcare/finance may require self-hosted infrastructure.

React hydration mismatch from dynamic content: rendering time-based content (new Date().toLocaleString()), random content, or user-specific content (localStorage) during SSR produces mismatches. The client renders different content than the server. React 18 recovers from mismatches by re-rendering client-side, but this eliminates SSR's benefit for that content. UNSAID: the three solutions: (1) suppressHydrationWarning on the element (React skips hydration mismatch check), appropriate for truly dynamic single values like timestamps; (2) render null on server, render actual value after mount (useEffect sets state after hydration); (3) use the value as a URL param or cookie readable on the server. Solution 2 is the most common — it's the 'client-only component' pattern: render a skeleton server-side, then replace with actual content after mounting.

React's choice of immutable updates (new object instead of mutation) aligns with its reconciliation algorithm: React uses reference equality (===) to determine if props/state changed. Mutable updates change the value in-place without changing the reference — React sees the same reference and skips the render. This is why `arr.push(item)` followed by setState(arr) doesn't trigger a re-render: React compares the array reference, which hasn't changed. UNSAID: this is the most fundamental React data model concept. Immer (produce function) bridges the gap — it allows mutation syntax while producing a new reference: `produce(state, draft => { draft.items.push(item) })`. Redux Toolkit uses Immer internally in createSlice reducers. Understanding this explains why Redux's reducer pattern works: `return { ...state, field: value }` creates a new reference, triggering React's re-render.

React native browser Dialog element vs Radix Dialog: HTML `<dialog>` element (2023 baseline, all modern browsers) provides built-in modal behavior: `showModal()` for modal mode, backdrop pseudo-element, Escape key handling, and focus trap. React can control it via ref.current.showModal(). UNSAID: the native `<dialog>` element is increasingly a viable alternative to Radix UI Dialog for simple use cases. It lacks some Radix features (animation out, portal rendering, composable API) but is zero-dependency. For design systems that need animation and complex composition, Radix remains superior. The accessibility of native `<dialog>` is better than custom div-based modals but still requires programmatic focus management for complex content within the dialog.

React component naming: components MUST start with a capital letter. Lowercase names (`function button() {}`) are treated as HTML elements — `<button>` renders an HTML button, `<Button>` renders your React component. TypeScript catches this in .tsx files. UNSAID: this is a parser-level distinction, not just a convention. The JSX transformer converts `<Button>` to `React.createElement(Button, ...)` (component reference) and `<button>` to `React.createElement('button', ...)` (HTML tag string). Violating this silently renders an HTML element with no functionality instead of your component — no error, just missing behavior. Named exports vs default exports for components: named exports are better for tree-shaking and prevent import name inconsistencies.

React with server-sent events (SSE): EventSource API for one-way server-to-client streaming. Pattern: `useEffect(() => { const es = new EventSource('/api/stream'); es.onmessage = (e) => setState(JSON.parse(e.data)); return () => es.close() }, [])`. SSE reconnects automatically on disconnect. Use cases: live notifications, real-time data feeds, AI streaming responses. UNSAID: SSE works over HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2, is firewall-friendly (regular HTTP), and automatically reconnects. WebSockets require a protocol upgrade and persistent connections. For read-only server-to-client streaming (live feeds, AI responses), SSE is simpler than WebSockets. Next.js experimental streaming with `ReadableStream` responses enables SSE-like patterns in API routes. The default browser limit of 6 concurrent SSE connections per domain requires HTTP/2 for production use with multiple streams.

React prop comparison in memo: React.memo uses Object.is() for prop comparison (same as ===). Objects and arrays are ALWAYS different references between renders unless explicitly stabilized. `<Memo component={{ key: 'value' }} />` — the inline object literal creates a new object reference every render, defeating memo. UNSAID: this is the memo trap that makes memo feel 'not working'. The fix: stabilize object/array props with useMemo or define them outside the component. `const STATIC_OPTIONS = { key: 'value' }` defined outside the component is stable. `useMemo(() => ({ key: value }), [value])` is stable when value is a primitive. The memo optimization only pays off when the stabilization cost (useMemo overhead) is less than the render cost saved.

React with Playwright for E2E testing: `page.getByRole()`, `page.getByText()`, `page.getByLabel()` queries mirror Testing Library's approach for consistency. Page Object Model (POM) pattern organizes complex test workflows into reusable classes. Playwright's codegen (`playwright codegen`) records interactions and generates test code. UNSAID: Playwright runs in 3 browsers (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit) with a single test suite. The component testing mode (`@playwright/experimental-ct-react`) is an emerging pattern — mounting React components in a real browser context for testing instead of jsdom. This bridges the gap between RTL (fast, jsdom) and full E2E (slow, real browser). Playwright's trace viewer records every action, network call, and screenshot for debugging CI failures.

React's composition model (passing components as props, children pattern) is more flexible than Vue/Angular's slot/content projection model. React children are just JavaScript — you can manipulate, filter, count, clone (React.Children API) children programmatically. UNSAID: the flexibility comes at a cost — React doesn't have named slots built-in. To pass multiple render regions (header, body, footer) to a layout component, you pass them as individual props: `<Layout header={<Header />} sidebar={<Sidebar />}>{content}</Layout>`. Vue slots are more ergonomic for this pattern; React's approach requires explicit prop naming. React.Children API (React.Children.map, cloneElement) should be used sparingly — it's the composition equivalent of reflection, powerful but can create hidden coupling.

React with OpenAI streaming: use SSE (server-sent events) or fetch with ReadableStream to stream AI responses word-by-word. Pattern: Server creates a TransformStream from OpenAI's streaming response; Client reads the stream with `reader = response.body.getReader()` and updates state incrementally. Vercel AI SDK (`ai` package) provides React hooks (`useChat`, `useCompletion`) that handle streaming, loading states, and abort. UNSAID: the Vercel AI SDK is the production standard for React + LLM streaming. `useChat` handles multi-turn conversations with streaming; `useCompletion` handles single completions. The SDK abstracts away ReadableStream parsing, reconnection, and cancellation. Multiple AI provider support (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini) via unified API. The `experimental_useObject` hook streams structured JSON objects (using Zod schema) — enables streaming partial objects before completion.

React 18 Concurrent Mode breaks existing code that assumes synchronous rendering. Patterns that break: (1) Storing component instance-level data in module-level variables (multiple concurrent renders corrupt each other), (2) Mutating Redux store directly (bypasses React's update scheduling), (3) Reading DOM state during render (DOM may not reflect current render in concurrent mode), (4) Non-idempotent render functions. UNSAID: React 18 with createRoot opts into concurrent mode. If upgrading from React 17, test all third-party libraries for concurrent mode compatibility. Libraries that use legacy lifecycle methods (UNSAFE_componentWillMount, UNSAFE_componentWillReceiveProps) may have concurrent mode bugs. The upgrade guide lists common issues and fixes.

React SEO for SPAs: Next.js is the standard solution — server-rendered HTML is crawled by search engines. For pure CSR React apps, dynamic rendering (Rendertron/Puppeteer serving pre-rendered HTML to bots) is the workaround. Metadata: Next.js generateMetadata function, Open Graph tags, JSON-LD structured data. UNSAID: Google can execute JavaScript (Googlebot can crawl CSR React), but with a delay of days to weeks for indexing. SSR/SSG with Next.js provides immediate indexing. For content-heavy sites (blogs, product pages, landing pages), SSR/SSG is not optional — delayed indexing directly impacts organic traffic. JSON-LD structured data (schema.org) provides rich snippets (star ratings, FAQ accordion) in search results — implemented as a `<script type='application/ld+json'>` tag.

React DevTools Profiler 'Ranked' chart shows components sorted by render time — the most expensive render at the top. Compare with 'Flamegraph' which shows hierarchy. UNSAID: the Ranked chart quickly identifies the top-5 slowest components without navigating the component tree. A component appearing frequently in the Ranked chart across multiple renders indicates a systematic re-render problem (not just a one-time expensive mount). Filtering by minimum render duration (> 1ms) reduces noise from fast renders. The 'interactions' panel shows which user interactions triggered which renders — correlating user actions with render patterns.

React with virtual scrolling and infinite query: combine TanStack Virtual (row virtualization) with TanStack Query's useInfiniteQuery (pagination). Virtual scroller renders only visible rows; infinite query fetches more pages as user scrolls near the bottom. Pattern: observe when virtual rows near the last item, trigger `fetchNextPage()`. UNSAID: the intersection between TanStack Virtual's last visible index and the data fetching threshold is the key integration point. If the fetch threshold is too early (fetch when 50% through current page), you may over-fetch. If too late (fetch when at last row), users see a loading state while scrolling. The sweet spot: fetch the next page when the user is 80% through the current page. isFetchingNextPage from useInfiniteQuery drives the loading indicator at the bottom.

React debugging a 'white screen of death' (blank page): (1) Check browser console for errors, (2) Check network tab for failed JS bundle loads, (3) Verify index.html has correct script src, (4) Check React.render / createRoot target element exists before React runs, (5) Add ErrorBoundary at root to catch rendering errors, (6) Check for SSR hydration errors in server logs. UNSAID: the most common cause of blank React pages in production: incorrect base path in Vite config (assets 404) or React.render called before DOM is loaded. The second most common: an unhandled error in the root component tree before ErrorBoundary exists. Adding `window.onerror = (msg, url, line) => console.error(msg, url, line)` to the HTML file captures errors before React initializes.

React useContext performance: using useContext for frequently-changing state (e.g., mouse position, scroll position, animation values) causes the entire consumer tree to re-render at 60fps — catastrophic performance. These values belong in refs (no re-render) or external libraries like react-spring/framer-motion that operate outside React's render cycle. UNSAID: the fundamental mismatch: React's render model is designed for UI updates at human interaction speed (100-300ms), not at browser animation frame rate (16ms). Animation state that triggers re-renders at 60fps will cause jank. The correct approach: drive animations via CSS transitions/animations (GPU-composited), or use animation libraries that update DOM directly without React re-renders (Framer Motion's layout animations, react-spring).

React with Stripe Checkout (hosted): redirect to Stripe's hosted checkout page for maximum conversion optimization and PCI compliance with zero UI work. Pattern: create Checkout Session server-side, return `url`, redirect client to Stripe URL. Success/cancel URLs bring the user back to your app. UNSAID: Stripe Checkout conversion rates are typically 5-10% higher than custom payment forms because Stripe continuously A/B tests and optimizes their checkout. The hosted checkout supports all Stripe payment methods, Link (one-click checkout), and adaptive pricing. For subscription billing, Stripe Customer Portal handles plan changes, cancellation, and invoice history — another hosted page that eliminates significant product work.

React 19 breaking changes summary: (1) ref is now a regular prop (forwardRef deprecated), (2) Context.Consumer is deprecated (use useContext), (3) ReactDOM.render is removed (use createRoot), (4) Some legacy lifecycle methods removed (UNSAFE_ warnings become errors), (5) String refs removed (use createRef/useRef). UNSAID: React 18 → React 19 migration is generally less disruptive than React 16 → React 17. The biggest practical change for most apps: updating from ReactDOM.render (already warned in React 18) to createRoot. Third-party libraries that still use string refs or Context.Consumer need updates. The React 19 migration guide provides codemods for the automated fixes.

React component documentation with TypeDoc and Storybook: TypeDoc generates API documentation from TypeScript types and JSDoc comments. Storybook provides interactive component explorer with controls (knobs). Combining both: TypeDoc for API reference, Storybook for visual documentation and interaction. UNSAID: for design systems, Storybook with addon-docs auto-generates documentation from TypeScript prop types — eliminating manual documentation maintenance. The ArgsTable component shows all props with types, defaults, and descriptions extracted from TypeScript. Chromatic's documentation feature publishes Storybook automatically on merge, providing always-current component documentation.

React's useEffect mental model: effects are for synchronizing React components with external systems (browser APIs, non-React libraries, servers). They are NOT for: computing derived state (use useMemo), responding to user events (use event handlers), or cascading state updates (restructure state or use event handlers). UNSAID: the core question before writing useEffect: 'What external system am I synchronizing with?' If the answer is 'nothing outside React', useEffect is wrong. The common anti-patterns that useEffect shouldn't solve: initializing non-React libraries (do this in event handlers or as module initialization), fetching data on every render (use TanStack Query), keeping two pieces of state in sync (derive one from the other, or lift to a reducer). The React docs 'You Might Not Need an Effect' guide covers every case systematically.

React context selector pattern (avoid full context re-renders): with use-context-selector library, `useContextSelector(Context, state => state.specificField)` only re-renders when the specific field changes. This provides Zustand-like performance characteristics for context-based state. UNSAID: use-context-selector is a workaround for a missing React feature. The React team has discussed a built-in context selector API but hasn't shipped it. In the meantime, use-context-selector is the pragmatic solution for high-frequency context updates affecting many consumers. The library uses a subscription model internally — each consumer subscribes to specific state slices and React schedules targeted re-renders. This works by bypassing React's default context propagation mechanism.

React TypeScript generic component pattern: components with type-parameterized props enable type-safe data tables, lists, and forms. `function List<T extends { id: string }>({ items, renderItem }: { items: T[]; renderItem: (item: T) => ReactNode }): JSX.Element`. The generic T inferred from items prop. UNSAID: TypeScript's inference for generic components in .tsx files has a syntax quirk — `<T>` is ambiguous (could be JSX). Fix: `<T extends unknown>` or `<T,>` (trailing comma) forces TypeScript to recognize it as a generic. In .ts files (non-JSX), no trailing comma needed. Generic components are most valuable for reusable data components (Table, Select, Combobox) where the data type varies but the behavior is consistent.

React concurrent features and Suspense: calling suspending components (those that throw Promises) inside event handlers will throw to the nearest error boundary, NOT suspend the component. Suspense only works during rendering phase. Calling `use(promise)` inside onClick will throw an unhandled error. UNSAID: this is a fundamental constraint of the Suspense model — it hooks into React's render scheduling, which only runs during the rendering phase. Async operations triggered by events must be handled differently: useTransition for deferring state updates, TanStack Query's useMutation for async mutations, or simply await in the event handler with local state for loading. The `use(promise)` hook is only valid during rendering — the compiler enforces this at build time in React 19.

React internationalization number/currency formatting: use built-in `Intl.NumberFormat` for locale-aware number formatting. `new Intl.NumberFormat('en-US', { style: 'currency', currency: 'USD' }).format(1234.56)` → '$1,234.56'. Memoize formatter instances (creating Intl.NumberFormat is expensive). UNSAID: react-i18next's `t('price', { val: 1234.56 })` with i18n number format configuration handles this consistently. The direct Intl.NumberFormat usage is sufficient for simple cases; i18next's integration is better when the number formatting rules vary by locale and need to be centrally configured. Relative time formatting (`Intl.RelativeTimeFormat`) handles '2 hours ago', '3 days ago' without a library — though date-fns's `formatDistanceToNow` is more ergonomic.

React form submission double-submit prevention: without disabling the submit button during pending, users can click multiple times and trigger duplicate submissions. Pattern: useFormState/isPending from React 19 useActionState, or TanStack Query's useMutation isLoading state disables the button. UNSAID: double-submit is both a UX problem and a correctness problem — especially for financial transactions (double charges), state mutations (duplicate records), and idempotency failures. The fix is always both: (1) UI disable during pending (prevents multiple clicks), AND (2) server-side idempotency (idempotency keys for APIs, unique constraints in database). UI-only protection fails for concurrent sessions (same user, two browser tabs).

React toast with context + reducer pattern (before sonner/react-hot-toast existed): ToastContext holds a list of toast messages, ToastProvider manages add/remove with useReducer, ToastContainer renders the stack in a portal, useToast hook exposes addToast. UNSAID: this DIY pattern is a learning exercise — in production, always use sonner or react-hot-toast. The DIY pattern's failure modes: (1) missing ARIA live region announcement for screen readers, (2) missing auto-dismiss with cleanup, (3) no deduplication. These are exactly what the libraries handle correctly. Building infrastructure components (toasts, modals, dropdowns) from scratch is low-value work that consumes time better spent on product features.

React internationalization setup with next-intl (Next.js): (1) Install next-intl, (2) Create messages directory with locale JSON files (en.json, zh.json), (3) Configure middleware for locale detection and routing, (4) Wrap layout with NextIntlClientProvider, (5) Use `useTranslations('namespace')` hook in components. UNSAID: next-intl is opinionated about routing — locale is in the URL path (/en/page vs /zh/page). This is SEO-optimal (each language has its own URL) but requires migrating existing routes. The format function `t('price', { price: 9.99 })` with ICU message format handles plurals and number formatting. Server Components use `getTranslations()` (async, returns translations for the current locale); Client Components use `useTranslations()`.

React's uncontrolled component and ref pattern bridges the gap between React's declarative model and the imperative browser API. Refs are the official escape hatch. Three legitimate uses: (1) DOM measurements and imperative DOM operations (focus, scroll, animate), (2) Storing mutable values that don't affect rendering (timer IDs, previous values), (3) Integrating with non-React libraries that need direct DOM access. UNSAID: refs are the least-React-idiomatic feature by design — they break out of React's data flow. Using refs for logic that could be expressed as state+props creates hidden state and makes components harder to test and compose. The proportion of refs to state in a component is a code smell indicator — more refs than state suggests imperative thinking in a declarative system.

React feature flag implementation with context and LaunchDarkly/Statsig: FeatureFlagContext provides flag values, useFeatureFlag hook reads specific flags. Server-side evaluation (Next.js): flags evaluated in Server Components using server-side SDK, passed as props to Client Components. Client-side: SDK initialized once, flags accessed via hooks. UNSAID: server-side flag evaluation prevents 'flicker' — Client Components with feature flags evaluated client-side show the default state briefly before the flag evaluates, causing a visible UI flash. Server-side evaluation eliminates this by rendering the correct state from the first byte. Statsig and LaunchDarkly both support server-side Next.js with edge-compatible SDKs. Bootstrap the client with server-evaluated flags to avoid the client-side fetch.

React key anti-pattern: resetting entire forms by changing the key prop is a legitimate pattern ('key as reset mechanism'), but placing the key too high in the tree resets unrelated components. Pattern: `<FormComponent key={resetCounter} />` — changing resetCounter forces a fresh mount, resetting all form state. UNSAID: this pattern is explicitly documented in React docs as a valid use case. The failure is OVER-applying it — putting a changing key on a parent component that contains unrelated children causes all children to remount unnecessarily. Scope the key to exactly the component you want to reset. Alternative for forms: RHF's `reset()` function resets the form without remounting, preserving focus and other non-form state.

React DevTools Components panel: search by component name (case-insensitive), filter by hooks used, highlight rendering components. The Components panel shows the full component tree — both Server and Client Components in React 19 with Next.js. UNSAID: the Components panel tree view can be overwhelming for large apps. The 'filter' option hides DevTools itself, context providers, and high-order components from the tree — cleaning up the view significantly. Inspecting component props in the panel allows real-time editing — change a prop value to see how the component responds. This is faster than modifying hardcoded values in source code for quick visual testing.

React with Zustand + Immer middleware: immer middleware allows mutation syntax in Zustand stores without external produce() calls. `create(immer((set) => ({ items: [], addItem: (item) => set(state => { state.items.push(item) }) })))`. State is automatically frozen with Immer — runtime mutations throw errors, catching bugs. UNSAID: Immer middleware makes Zustand particularly ergonomic for nested state updates. Without Immer: `set(state => ({ nested: { ...state.nested, field: value } }))`. With Immer: `set(state => { state.nested.field = value })`. The productivity win compounds with deep nesting. Performance consideration: Immer adds overhead proportional to object size — for stores with large arrays (10,000+ items), benchmark before adopting.

React re-export pattern in component libraries: re-exporting all components from a barrel file (index.ts: `export * from './Button'; export * from './Input'; ...`) enables clean imports (`import { Button, Input } from '@/components/ui'`) but can break tree-shaking in webpack setups without sideEffects:false in package.json. UNSAID: Vite's rollup-based tree-shaking handles barrel files correctly in most cases. The real problem: if the barrel file contains side effects (module-level code that runs on import), the entire barrel is included in the bundle even if you only use one export. `sideEffects: false` in package.json tells bundlers all re-exports are pure. Deep imports (`import Button from '@/components/ui/Button'`) always tree-shake correctly as a fallback.

React experimental 'Activity' component (formerly Offscreen): allows rendering a component without displaying it — pre-renders inactive routes for instant navigation. When the component becomes visible, it resumes from the pre-rendered state. UNSAID: Activity is the foundation for instant tab switching and pre-rendered navigation in React. It's available in React 19 as an experimental API. The mental model: Activity is like display:none but React-aware — the component maintains its state (unlike unmount/remount) but doesn't paint. Timer effects run in a low-priority mode when hidden. This enables pre-rendering tab content without the full overhead of keeping the component mounted in the normal tree.

React app accessibility audit workflow: (1) axe DevTools browser extension — automated scan for WCAG violations, (2) Keyboard-only navigation test (unplug mouse, navigate via Tab/Enter/Space/Escape/Arrow keys), (3) Screen reader test (VoiceOver on macOS: Cmd+F5 to activate, navigate with VO+arrow), (4) Color contrast check (WCAG Contrast Checker, min 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text), (5) Focus indicator visible (outline must be visible in all interactive states), (6) Form label association (every input has a label), (7) ARIA usage audit (no aria-label on non-interactive elements, proper roles). UNSAID: accessibility audits should happen in CI (jest-axe) AND at intervals during development (manual keyboard/screen reader). Automated tools catch ~30-40% of issues; manual testing catches the remainder.

React's decision to make DOM event handling synthetic (wrapping native events) historically enabled: cross-browser normalization, event pooling (React 16), and delegation to a single root listener. In React 17+, pooling was removed and delegation changed from document to root. The synthetic event system is now mostly transparent — it wraps native events with minor normalizations (onClick works the same on all browsers). UNSAID: the synthetic event system is why you call e.preventDefault() on synthetic events, not nativeEvent.preventDefault(). Both work, but e is the synthetic wrapper. e.nativeEvent gives access to the underlying browser event if needed for browser-specific APIs. The event naming convention (camelCase: onClick, onChange, onKeyDown) is React-specific — different from HTML's lowercase attributes (onclick, onchange).

React server component data patterns: `async function Component() { const data = await prisma.user.findMany(); return <UserList users={data} /> }` — directly queries database with zero client JavaScript for the data fetching. Caching with React's `cache()` deduplicates identical queries within a render pass. UNSAID: this is architecturally significant — React Server Components enable the query-to-UI pattern that was previously only possible in PHP/Rails. The entire request/response cycle for server-rendered data: URL → server component → database query → HTML response. No API routes, no client state, no loading states. The security benefit: database credentials never reach the client, query logic stays server-side. For dynamic personalized data (user-specific content), server components + cookies() is cleaner than client fetching.

React 19 + Next.js 15 adoption timing: App Router is stable but ecosystem libraries may lag. Libraries built for Pages Router (next-auth v4, some tRPC versions) require adapter layers for App Router. The React 19 concurrent features and Server Components require libraries to explicitly support them. UNSAID: checking library compatibility with App Router before starting a project avoids mid-project rewrites. Key ecosystem libraries status (2024): Next-Auth v5 (Auth.js) supports App Router; tRPC v11 supports RSC; Prisma supports Server Components; TanStack Query v5 supports Server Components. The Canary releases of React/Next.js test the latest features — production apps should use stable releases unless specific canary features are required.

React with Recoil (Facebook's atomic state management): atoms are units of state, selectors derive state from atoms. `atom({ key: 'userAtom', default: null })`, `useRecoilState(userAtom)`. Recoil is React 18 concurrent mode compatible and supports asynchronous selectors (suspense-compatible). UNSAID: Recoil has not reached v1.0 stability as of 2024 and is largely maintained by community. Jotai (Recoil-inspired, maintained by the pmndrs community) is the recommended alternative with better maintenance and smaller bundle size. Jotai's `atom` → `useAtom(atom)` pattern is nearly identical to Recoil but with a smaller footprint. For new projects, Jotai or Zustand are preferred over Recoil due to active maintenance.

React import cycle detection: circular imports in React components cause hard-to-debug runtime errors where components are undefined at import time. Symptom: component renders null unexpectedly, or 'X is not a function' error at import. UNSAID: JavaScript resolves circular imports with a partial import — the imported value may be undefined at the time of the import. ESLint's `import/no-cycle` rule detects circular imports at development time. The fix: move shared types/utilities to a separate file, or reorganize the dependency direction (parent doesn't import child's types). The barrel file `index.ts` pattern is the most common source of import cycles in React apps — components importing from the barrel that also exports them.

React with AI-powered UI generation: v0.dev (Vercel), Figma-to-code plugins, and Claude/Copilot all generate React+Tailwind+shadcn components from natural language or designs. The generated code is a starting point — always review for accessibility, error handling, and edge cases. UNSAID: AI-generated React code consistently produces: missing error states, missing empty states, no loading skeletons, missing mobile responsiveness. These gaps are systematic — AI optimizes for the happy path. The review checklist for AI-generated React: (1) Does it handle loading? (2) Does it handle errors? (3) Does it handle empty data? (4) Is it accessible (ARIA, keyboard)? (5) Does it work on mobile? Adding these five states doubles the code but makes it production-ready.
